**THE PACT WORLDS**

**SUBSYSTEMS**

**ANACHRONISTIC ADVENTURES**

entities, or artificial intelligences, that have convinced people to worship them.

**Limited:** Deities exist, though they remain aloof from the mortal world and make their divine presence known only to a chosen few.

**Accepted:**Divine influence is an accepted fact of everyday life. Their will is enacted through priests and organized religions. Divine avatars might appear in the world during extreme circumstances.

**Ubiquitous:** Deities live among mortals, exerting their divine will directly. Gods frequent social media sites, run streaming broadcasts to their followers, or even dominate entire solar systems as rulers.

### **Magic**

Does magic exist? If so, which traditions are available? What are the sources of a spellcaster's power, and how do they gain and channel that magic?

**No** Magic: Magic of any kind doesn't exist in this world. Spells and magic effects don't function. Consider the variants on page 82 to handle the lack of magic items or convert magic items to tech items using such advanced technology as to be indistinguishable from magic.

**Low Magic:** Magic is mysterious and taboo. The few practitioners of the mystical arts are feared or shunned. Again, consider the variants on page 82 to handle the relative scarcity of magic items.

**Common:** Magic is an accepted fact of everyday life, though, like computer programming, most users don't bother learning the details of how it works. Magic portals and gates can whisk travelers "in the know" halfway across the world or to the other side of the multiverse.

**High Magic:** Magic and magical items are commonplace in society, perhaps even more than technology. It might be as easy to learn spellcasting as it is to learn a new language. Many tech objects are replaced with magic objects that cost the same and serve the same function, like handheld magic mirrors instead of comm units. The fantastic is never more than a stone's throw away.

# **THE MULTIVERSE**

In Starfinder, the physical universe of your world is one plane within a much broader multiverse. The Planes, on page 182, details how planes work and the multiverse of Starfinder, but you can fit planes into your story and world, or even build a new multiverse from scratch! Perhaps there are only two planes beyond the material Universe, diametrically opposed and fighting over mortal souls, or the multiverse consists only of a series of infinite alternate realities. The options are truly endless, limited only by your imagination and the story you want to tell.

# **| RELIGION**

The greatest stories from myth and legend speak of immortals with incredible powers of creation and destruction. Some meddle in the affairs of mortals, shaping heroes and

history at a whim, while others remain aloof or oblivious to the mortal world. Regardless of the world you're building, religion (or even the absence thereof) shapes the characters and the stories you tell.

# **THEOLOGY**

Religious traditions are commonly categorized by their belief in one or more divine entities.

**Polytheism:** This belief system posits the existence of many gods. Polytheistic gods typically espouse particular areas of concern and often reflect the appearance of their worshippers. The primary religious philosophy of Starfinder is polytheistic.

**Dualism:** This philosophy espouses an enduring conflict between two diametrically opposed cosmic forces; most commonly good and evil or law and chaos. Acolytes of each faith almost always see themselves as righteous, and those of the contrasting belief as false. Some philosophies fuse cosmic duality into a whole. The philosophical cosmic balance of the Cycle focuses on the dual nature of creation and destruction.

**Monotheism:** A monotheistic doctrine recognizes the existence of only one true god, such as the Jinsul Hierocracy and their supposed devotion to KadricaL The supreme deity might exhibit more than one aspect yet remain a single entity, like Zon-Shelyn from Starfinder.

**Pantheism:** Divine power arises from the universe itself or as a byproduct of the collective power of many deities sharing some common facet, either way forming a vast, allencompassing divine entity. Worshippers sometimes appeal to or devote themselves to specific fundamental concepts or aspects of the universe.

**Animism:** Rather than worshipping gods associated with souls and spiritual essence from beyond, animism sees the life force in each part of the world, whether it be the trees of an old-growth forest or a towering waterfall.

**Singularitism:** In high-tech campaign worlds, some believe that all life will merge into a vast technological collective hive mind. Believers in machine-life evolution, like the Mechanizers, might see it as an inevitable path all life will eventually pursue. Others believe that life's purpose is creating a perfect Al that will guide life toward machine perfection. While most tend to acknowledge this long-term goal as one that can't be forced, more radical believers might take an aggressive approach, forcefully converting organic life-forms into cyborg bodies.

**Atheism: In** some campaign worlds, the gods have all died, abandoned their worshippers, or never existed at all. Mortals of this world might still cling to belief and establish religions in the name of the divine, but there are no true deities to answer their prayers.

# **PANTHEONS**

In polytheistic traditions, a pantheon is a divine hierarchy of multiple (or even all) deities.

**Universal:** All deities in the setting belong to a single pantheon. Different cultures might have their own names

for the god of magic, for instance, but only a single deity answers their prayers.

**Ancestral or Regional:** Each ancestry or region worships its own distinct pantheon. These pantheons coexist in the same cosmology but establish control in separate divine realms. Across the cosmos, several gods from disparate pantheons might share the same area of concern, but they seldom compete for worshippers from rival pantheons. They might also share the same infosphere sites or social media platforms, combining followers by sharing broadcasts.

**Competitive:** The world contains smaller regional pantheons competing for mortal worship. Only one deity of a specific area of concern may ascend to greater power across all the pantheons. As such, deities typically have little loyalty to their own pantheons and might actually switch to another pantheon if it earns them additional worshippers.

**None:**The deities of this multiverse act as individuals with no familial ties or common agendas binding them to each other.

# DEITIES

These immortal beings command vast power and influence fueled by the faith and souls of mortal worshippers. In Starfinder, deities also dictate some of the abilities of those who channel their power. When designing deities, you'll need to include the divine statistics and devotee benefits described below.

### Diuine Rank

Gods are usually ranked in a divine hierarchy, from newly ascended godlings to almighty creator gods of unfathomable power. Sometimes, a deity might enter a form of torpor and their power might diminish, requiring them to gather new followers to reascend.

**God:** Taking a position atop the divine pyramid, gods command near unlimited power and resources. Their mortal congregations are large and (usually) well funded.

**Demigod:** Demigods still possess a great deal of power, though often in subservience to another god or simply inferior to the power of a full god.

Quasi Deity: The weakest rank of divinity, many quasi deities are recently ascended mortals who attained their deific powers through ritual apotheosis, or planar natives who have amassed divine power of their own.

Artificial Deity: These virtual gods are supercomputers capable of acting out seemingly implausible miracles within the limited scope of their physical network. They can often be deleted or programmed by gaining physical access to their mainframes, which consist of entire demiplanes.

# Dii/int? Statistics

In Starfinder, deities are not only a narrative eiement of the world, but also a mechanical component of some characters.

**Areas of Concern:** Each deity has one or more areas of concern they have divine influence over. These portfolios typically embrace universal concepts, such as honor, night, or tranquility. Deities with similar areas of concern might

work in common cause or against each other, depending on their goals and divine rank.

**Edicts:** Every deity has edicts, which are those tenets they require their faithful to promote in the world. A deity usually has one to three simple and straightforward edicts.

**Anathema:** The opposite of edicts, anathema are those things a deity won't abide. Divine mystics usually avoid their deity's anathema, and even lay worshippers usually feel guilty for performing such acts, as they'll be weighed against them in the afterlife. Like edicts, a deity usually has two to three simple and straightforward elements to their anathema.

# Deizotee Benefits

Deities grant favored status and special power to the most fervent and influential of their flock.

**Cleric Spells:** When preparing spells, clerics from Pathfinder can choose from specific spells granted by the deity, in addition to those available on the divine spell list. A deity always grants a1st-rank spell and usually two others, all chosen from non-divine spell lists. The exact number of spells a deity grants can vary-a magic-focused deity might grant more-though this shouldn't exceed one spell of each rank.

**Divine Font:** Clerics from Pathfinder channel a deity's divine power as a font of vitality or void energy. Most deities grant either heo/ or harm, but a few deities offer a choice between the two. A specific deity's divine font should be based on their areas of concern.

**Divine Sanctification:** Some deities allow or require their greatest devotees to dedicate themselves to pursuing holy or unholy deeds. A listing of "must choose holy" or "must choose unholy" indicates the deity requires this commitment. "Can choose holy <sup>n</sup> or "can choose unholy" indicates that the deity allows it in addition to many other options.

**Divine** Skill: Champions and clerics from Pathfinder automatically gain the trained proficiency rank in their deity's divine skill. Assign the deity one skill that synergizes well with their areas of concern. For example, Intimidation would be appropriate for a god of tyranny, or Deception for a goddess of trickery.

**Domains:** Each deity grants a number of domains that reflect their divine areas of concern. Mystics can learn the domain spells from their deity's domains. Starfinder's deities each have four domains, and many have one or more alternative domains. Though this number is usually enough to convey a deity's portfolio and give players sufficient options, you can give your deities as many domains as you like.

**Favored Weapon:**Some characters can gain access to their deity's favored weapon as well as the trained proficiency rank with it. Theseweapons are considered specific to the religion's culture for purposes of feats like the shirren's Unconventional Weaponry. Every deity has a favored weapon. Because the benefits of having an advanced favored weapon are very strong, you should assign simple or martial favored weapons unless a deity is so thematically linked with an advanced weapon that you need to give them one.

**BUILDING SETTLEMENTS**

**THE PACT WORLDS**

**SUBSYSTEMS**

**ANACHRONISTIC ADVENTURES**

tn Starfinder, settlements are where characters can rest, recharge, retrain, and dedicate themselves to other downtime activities, all in relative peace. Traditionally, an adventuring band comes together in some kind of settlement, be it a small colony recently established on a moon in the Vast or a bustling continent-spanning city on the surface of a densely populated world. Some adventures take place entirely within a single settlement, while in others the party visits settlements only briefly between their adventures in space.

The first thing you should consider when building a settlement is its role in your story. Is this a major metropolis the heroes will visit again and again during their adventures? A backwater outpost where their adventures begin? The distant interstellar capital from which an evil tyrant issues cruel edicts? The settlement's campaign role will inform many of the other decisions you make about the place.

Once you know why you need the settlement, consider why it would exist in the setting. While established settlements certainly need access to sundries and supplies, local access to these resources are only necessary in low-tech or isolated outposts. Metropolises can even exist on worlds devoid of any naturally occurring food or water, although they're likely populated largely by creatures who don't need to eat or drink. These settlements need to be in such a valuable location that it offsets their need to import necessary supplies. While it might be easier to create a space station or city merely to serve the characters' needs, determining what function it has independent of the characters adds verisimilitude and can provide hooks for further stories.

Settlement stat blocks describe the components and traits of a settlement. You don't need to create a settlement stat block, but doing so can help flesh out the community you're building, and they can be a useful reference for your game. For examples of settlement stat blocks, see page 82 of Ga/oxy Guide.

# **MAPPING A SETTLEMENT**

Don't underestimate the usefulness of sketching a map of significant settlements, like the one where your adventure starts. This isn't intended to be a picture-perfect rendition drawn to scale, but rather to outline the rough shape and size of the settlement. Be sure to highlight a few key structures useful to the campaign.

Step 1 Layout: The layout of a settlement is as unique as the terrain upon which it's settled. In settlements developed

before the advent of spaceflight, you might want to look at major trade routes likely developed alongside rivers. In more modern cities, this usually means looking at where the city's spaceport is located, likely near a body of water for emergency landings or where land would be cheap after the advent of spaceflight. The settlement might also use a structure like a space elevator, which likely makes its anchor a solid hub. If your settlement is a space station, build the central core and expand outward.

**Step 2. Districts:** Archaic settlements likely have a central district once defended by a wall. In some settlements, this area might be the most expensive part of town, filled with palaces and tourist districts, allowing guests a look at years gone by. If the settlement was established during or after an industrial revolution, the old town might very well be a run-down and polluted den of criminal activity. Districts often naturally develop as settlements grow into adjacent settlements, incorporating the smaller town or village into the larger settlement These different wards often specialize over time into the dominant industry of the settlement, with residential districts typically replacing agrarian plots in postscarcity societies.

**Step 3. Malls and Markets:** Designate one or more open spaces in the settlement as a major commercial space. This marketplace typically grows in the city's downtown, often on the ground floor of office buildings or in large shopping malls. Mid-sized cities often develop these hearts of commerce in marketplaces and bazaars sometimes thousands of years old, using temporary stalls for local food and small businesses alongside large interstellar corporations in newly opened strip malls. This setup gives adventurers the flexibility to buy and deal in exotic and ill-gotten gains while still being able to reliably purchase mass-produced equipment from major manufacturers.

**Step 4. Lodging:** Heroes need a place to celebrate and recover between adventures. Bars, clubs, and restaurants make ideal locations to rest, introduce notable NPCs, and initiate quests. Unlike the medieval tavern, the hottest locations rarely have lodgings of their own but are often in districts featuring hotels or close enough to the spaceport that you can always save some credits bunking in the party's starship. Some cities feature alternative lodgings, ranging from sleeping pods and overnight cyber cafes to hostels and house rentals.

**Step5. Landmarks:** Togive your cities a sense of personality and local flavor, design a handful of iconic landmarks for the PCs to visit. Memorable names make these landmarks more interesting and can help hint toward future themes and story beats. A random stellar observatory might be noteworthy, but the Tapestry's Eye has an air of intrigue that could lead to a fun adventure hook.

# DETAILING SETTLEMENTS

It's important to have some idea of a settlement's culture established. While every nuanced detail of its governing laws and cultural norms can often be hand-waved, you'll want a minimal description of the core concept of your settlement, including its style of government and core cultural edicts and anathema. The amount of additional detail you provide depends on the needs of your story. If you have multiple governing bodies involved in your campaign, especially if your story includes economic or political intrigue, you'll want to establish enough information to create reference sheets for your players to serve as helpful reminders of all the relevant factions in your campaign. That said, many settlements in a multi-planetary campaign function fairly independent of any larger governing body, their autonomy assured strictly by the extensive internal bureaucracy necessary to maintain a vibrant multicultural metropolis of millions.

Beyond those basic details, the following considerations can help flesh out the settlements in your setting.

# Location, Size, and Population

Major planetary geographical boundaries, such as mountains, seas,and large rivers, often present natural borders for a region. Depending on its leadership, culture, and the resources available, a settlement might be as small as an areology floating over a gas giant or as large as a planet-spanning megaplex. In Starfinder, most settlements tend to be larger than the historical cities of Lost Golarion and might cover hundreds or thousands of square miles or span an entire planet. Widespread technological and magical innovations give governing entities wider reach, improve city maintenance, and foster cultural mixing. These settlements are often surrounded by greater metropolitan areas,which might or might not be considered part of the settlement itself.

Populations ebb and flow due to a multitude of external factors. Advances in sanitation, medicine, and agriculture can spur dramatic population growth, while war, economic downturn, or plague can devastate it. A megacity has at least 10 million residents. Megaplexes formed of multiple megacities might have populations averaging 50 million or more. A multi-planetary alliance might have billions or more.

Population size is only part of the equation. Figuring out the ancestry ratios of that population and brainstorming how the members of various ancestries interact can often lead to interesting story ideas, or at least give you some jumping-off points when dreaming up how the settlement was founded and its later history.

### Cultural Hallmarks

What elements of the settlement's predominant culture

# **CONSCIENTIOUS CULTUREDESIGN**

Creating science-fantasy cultures can be a delicate topic. It's best to avoid creating species or cultures based on damaging stereotypes or simplistic reductions of real-world groups. Creating entire cultures out of harmful genre tropes, such as "primitive nomad" or "noble samurai,'' reduces real-world cultures to caricatures and perpetuates misconceptions about people from those cultures. Taking inspiration from the real world is wonderful-our world is filled with an incredible diversity of cultures and peoples-but you should do so with care and respect for those sources.

stand out? A settlement might have an unusual stance on religion, a specific demographic, distinctive natural features, noteworthy political views, or any number of unique elements that differentiate it from other settlements in the galaxy. These hallmarks can inform your decisions about many other aspects of the settlement.

**CREATURES** History How did the settlement come to be? Has it stood since before the Gap, a bastion of stability while the rest of the galaxy changed around it? Perhaps it was built over the ruins of another civilization that was destroyed eons ago by powerful invaders still traveling the distant stars. Or perhaps it's a young settlement, founded recently by refugees seeking to escape the Swarm. Is it the first settlement on a planet of untamed wilderness or a city of refugees built on the same asteroid that ruined a once-thriving metropolitan world? How have the residents of the settlement adapted to change, and in what ways have they failed to do so?

### **ADVENTURES** Economy and Political Stances

Determine the key resources and industries that drive the settlements economy. The availability of natural resources, skills and specializations of its citizens, and relations with its neighbors can all be factors in determining a settlement's economic success. For example, an area with few resources might be more reliant on technological innovation or military prowess, while a settlement rich in resources will more likely develop an opulent upper class composed of those with the capital to exploit such resources.

These resources can also affect political relationships. An area poor in a specific resource might have a strong trade relationship with another world that has it, or they might rely on conquest and war to plunder what they need! Settlements also disagree about political structures, public policy, religion, and any number of other factors.

You'll also want to consider the significant NPCs of each settlement.This includes the official rulers, but it also includes other major players, whether they act in an official capacity orentirely behind the scenes.

# **CHAPTER 3: THG PACT WORLDS INTRODUCTION**

Three centuries ago, the universal event now known as the Gap erased an entire era from history. Records for this missing time are scrambled or nonexistent, and people who lived through the Gap experienced a collective amnesia about its events. Neither magic nor technology can bridge this broken timeline, and although the gods must have some understanding of what happened, they refuse to divulge any information. The Gap (and the events it no doubt obscures] is the greatest mystery in the history of the galaxy.

During the Gap, civilizations changed and even vanished. The planet **Golarion** disappeared, and **AbsalomStation** appeared in its place, with the fabled **Starstone** fused to a reactor at its core. Lives and societies were upended, and in the aftermath of this chaos, all the inhabitants of the galaxy grappled for identity, stability, and hope, banding together for survival or clashing over resources and power. Life endured, and new beliefs, cultures, societies, and governments began to influence the galaxy as a new age of gods and heroes emerged.

A few years after the Gap,the artificial god Triune dispatched the **Signal,** which gifted civilizations across the galaxy the innovation of hyperspace travel through the mysterious dimension known as the **Drift.** Triune's church maintains magical infrastructure called **Drift beacons**that enable ships to navigate the Drift and send messages across the galaxy. Using a **Drift engine,**people throughout the galaxy can travel vast interstellar distances in a few days or weeks. Journeys to Absalom Station are always swift because of the Starstone. Drift travel has connected the galaxy in ways unimagined in the past and cemented Absalom Station as the center of the known galaxy.

The years after the Signal were an era of galactic exploration, which resulted in rapid exchange of knowledge, culture, religion, and people as well as the exploitation of resources and unclaimed worlds. Conflict inevitably followed, both within and across solar systems. The planets of the Golarion System banded together for mutual protection, forming the **Pact** Worlds System, after a vicious battle against the Veskarium. an empire from **Near Space.** Skirmishes between these two powers continued for over two and a half centuries, until peace was brokered in the face of a more powerful threat: the vicious, world-devouring insectile forces of the Swarm. Today, the Swarm ravages a magical republic called the **Szandite Collective** in Near Space, while the Veskarium is at war with the tyrannical **Azianti Star Empire.**

Every Pact World has its own military. A mutual defense force of battle-trained diplomats known as the **Stewards** protect the Pact Worlds from outside threats and upholds the treaty bonding these worlds together. They recruit from all over the allied planets and protectorates united by the Pact, operating out of their Bastion on Absalom Station.

A few years ago, a galactic catastrophe called the **Drift Crisis** occurred, shattering the status quo and throwing the galaxy into turmoil as the Drift suddenly malfunctioned, spitting out starships randomly across space and into other planes of existence, then failing completely. While the Drift soon stuttered back to functionality, it remained unstable and incredibly dangerous for nearly a year. During that time, a group of adventures, now revered as folk heroes, rebooted the Drift and stabilized the dimension for continued travel. Hyperspace highways known as **Drift lanes** emerged because of this event, connecting major ports and making trips even faster.

Peace in the Pact Worlds endures, but with numberless threats in a vast universe, danger and adventure are always possible. It's an exciting time to explore Starfinder!

# **| THE PACT WORLDS**

The galaxy is full of wonder and peril, advanced technology, magic, meddling gods, and countless new worlds to explore. It's a science-fantasy setting, combining elements of science fiction with those of classic fantasy while taking inspiration from the modern world. Although infinite opportunities for adventure await on other planets, systems, and planes, the Pact Worlds are the focus of the Starfinder campaign setting. With 13 major worlds, dozens of moons, and countless nations, cities, frontiers, wildlands, and strange phenomena, this group of allied planets present a huge range of opportunities for heroism and villainy, drama and disgrace, fame and fortune!

Because Absalom Station is easy to reach from anywhere in the galaxy, the Pact Worlds makes a perfect home base for interstellar explorers and travelers, safe from the horrors and dangers of the wider galaxy. It's the heart of the Starfinder setting and a collection of places worth protecting.

The Pact Worlds' core system is a group of planets, major satellites, and regions orbiting lost Golarion's sun. These united worlds are Aballon, Castrovel, Absalom Station. Akiton, Verces, the Diaspora. Eox, Triaxus, Liavara, Bretheda, and Apostae, plus the Brethedan moons of Dykon, Kalo-Mahoi, and Marata. The *Idari-a* generation ship built and inhabited by kasathas-is a Pact World, as is Pulonis, a planet in Near Space formerly

# **LOSTGOLARION**

The solar system now called the Pact Worlds has been inhabited for millennia, dating back long before the Gap to the beginnings of recorded history. While all the planets in the system are significant, none present as fascinating a mystery as Golarion, the world that vanished without a trace.

Golarion is the presumed birthplace of humans, ysoki, dwarves, goblins, halflings, hobgoblins, orcs, and numerous other ancestries still found in the Pact Worlds today. Despite this, Golarion was most notable not for its varied civilizations and peoples, but for its theological significance: Golarion's core served as the prison for a long-forgotten god of destruction named **Rovagug.** Doubly blessed and cursed, Golarion was also home to a miraculous artifact called the Starstone, which enabled worthy mortals to achieve divinity by passing the stone's tests. This stone birthed multiple deities, including **lomedae.** who's still worshipped by many humans and ritually honored by members of the **Knights of Golarion** in the modern age. It's commonly believed the Starstone at the heart of Absalom Station is the very same Starstone that was once located on Lost Golarion, though no one has used the modern Starstone to ascend to divinity since the Gap, leaving fertile ground for conspiracy theories to take root.

At some point during the Gap, Golarion disappeared, and Absalom Station appeared in its place. Scientific inquiry, magical divinations, and religious communion have revealed only a single fact: the planet Golarion still exists, as do the descendants of those people on Golarion when it vanished, safe in seclusion unreachable by magic or science. Beyond this kernel of truth-or perhaps misinformation-no further information is forthcoming. At least, not yet...

For more information on the planet of Lost Golarion and its ancient past, see Pathfinder, and especially Pathfinder Lost Omens.

occupied by the Veskarium. What's left of Aucturn, a planet destroyed by the birth of a god, floats among the field of ice and haunted debris ringing the system's outskirts, called the Gelid Edge. Pages 152-177 detail the planets and regions of the Pact Worlds System, including their people, governments, cultures, and themes. Only a brief overview of each planet is presented here-enough to establish the setting and spark the imagination. If you're interested in exploring the Pact Worlds and the wider galaxy further, see the Starfinder Ga/axy Guide, Starfinder Adventures, and Starfinder Society Scenarios.

At time of print, the year in the Pact Worlds is 325 After Gap (AG). As the calendar advances in the real world, time also progresses for the Pact Worlds. Starfinder P/ayer Core was first published in the year 2025, with the Pact Worlds<sup>1</sup>

corresponding year ending in the same two final digits. The Pact Worlds' history is expansive, but four of the most significant events to shape the world occurred in 0 **AG.** when the Gap erased history and the modern era began: in 3 AG when Triune's Signal led to the discovery of the Drift and Drift travel; 41 AG when the Pact Worlds unified; and 324 AG when the Pact Worlds planet **Aucturn** hatched, birthing an outer godling dubbed the **Newborn** into the universe and forming the **Gelid Edge** at the fringes of the Pact Worlds.

The map on page 148 depicts the Pact Worlds System, which features the planets and regions explored on the following pages.

# I BEYOND THE PACT WORLDS

The Pact System is but a single solar system within the Desna's Path galaxy. Countless other systems are spread across space including the Veskarium and the Szandite Collective in Near Space, and the Azlanti Star Empire in the Vast. Some threats lurk on the fringes of known space, like the Swarm and the shadowy puppeteers inside the Riven Shroud, and realms wait to be discovered beyond charted space.

# NEAR SPACE

Near Space is a term used by people in the galaxy for all worlds whose nearness to Drift beacons makes travel swift and relatively safe. People in the Pact Worlds refer to places like the Marixah Republic and Daimalko as Near Space, but people on those worlds commonly call the Pact Worlds Near Space.

Communication, trade, and travel between Near Space systems is common, and a journey to a location in Near Space typically takes a week or so (3d6 days). This ease of travel puts all of Near Space within easy reach of explorers, traders, and threats emerging from these regions.

The most prominent force in Near Space is the **Veskarium.** a mighty interstellar empire of vesk and all the peoples they've conquered. Occupying the entire **GhavaniskaSystem** plus a few more distant colonies, the Veskarium is an expansionist empire currently at peace with the Pact Worlds and fighting a war with the more powerful Azlanti Star Empire. The patron deity of the Veskarium is the war god **Damoritosh** [page 200).

Other major powers in Near Space include the **Marixah Republic,** a democratic federation, and the **Gideron Authority,** a militarized coalition of worlds. These stellar neighbors both share ancient ties to lost Golarion and frequently quarrel over policy and territory; while they currently maintain a tentative peace, war might be on the horizon. The **Szandite Collective** is a federation of seven worlds spanning four separate star systems, all linked by ancient magical crystals called szandite. The Collective is currently under attack by the Swarm. Notable independent planets in Near Space include colossi-ravaged **Daimalko.** infernally-bound **Embroi.** polluted **Tabrid Minor,** and **Preluria,** a gas giant concealing numerous anarchic settlements and bickering factions in its orbital rings.

For more information on the Veskarium and the other worlds of Near Space, see page 178.

# THE VOST

The Vast includes everything in the galaxy outside the Drift beacon-dense region of Near Space. The worlds and locations in these regions are dangerous, infrequently traveled, and often uncharted, making the Vast both a hot spot for exploration and a perilous wasteland. Travel to locations in the Vast typically takes a few weeks to a month [5d6 days], and destinations frequently include isolated planets and civilizations, uncharted worlds, fledgling colonies, mining enterprises, ancient ruins, stellar anomalies, and vast unexplored regions of space.

The most prominent force in the Vast is the **Azlanti Star Empire,** a tyrannical, human-centric regime centered on **New Thespera** and the **Aristia System,** that has conquered over 11 other solar systems through expansive conquest and colonization. After a sudden coup toppled the longruling **Ixomander** dynasty, the Azlanti Star Empire launched a military assault on the world of **Kehtaria.** sparking war between the Azlanti Star Empire and the Veskarium. The patron god of the Azlanti Star Empire is **Lissala,** goddess of duty, obedience, and the rewards of service (page 201].

Other regions of interest include the **Scoured Stars,** a trinary system recently explored by the **Starfinder Society** and under the protective purview of the jealous god **Kadrical;** the **Jinsul Hierocracy,** a xenophobic war machine of vicious jinsuls intent on scouring the galaxy of all sentient life not originating in the Scoured Stars; and **Kazmurg's Absurdity,** a recently opened sector of space where interstellar travel is broken, forcing explorers to rely on old-fashioned maps and star charts to access new locations. Other sites of note include: the planet **Eiytrio,** a radioactive wasteland: **Lajok.** a world of mysterious ruins orbiting a reborn sun; and the **Shadari Confederacy,** a coalition of seven planets and haven for criminals near the fringes of the Azlanti Star Empire.

For more information on the Azlanti Star Empire and the other worlds of the Vast, see page 180.

# THE GOLOXV

Desna's Path is a vast galaxy, with a limitless number of locations to visit, discover, and explore. Yet, there are countless other galaxies within the universe, each as expansive and exciting as the Pact Worlds' own. To date, there have been no confirmed instances of intergalactic travel. Whether it's due to the extreme travel times involved, limits of the Drift itself, or dangers encountered en route during such attempts remains unknown. For now, Desna's Path is the only galaxy accessible to the citizens of the Pact Worlds.

# **| THE GREAT BEYOND**

Countless planets lie beyond Desna's Path, but even these myriad worlds of **the Universe** are but a fragment compared to what lies in the multiverse beyond. Other planes of existence and strange dimensions wrap this reality in a series of layered, nested spheres, known collectively as the **Great Beyond.** This model of reality is as much a metaphor

# **TIME**

Absalom Station has an artificial day/night cycle lasting 24 hours, similar to Earth's. A week has 7 days and a year has 52 weeks. To keep the calendar synchronized with the astronomical year, an extra leap day is tacked on to the second month of the year every 4 years. Despite the different orbits and cycles of other planets, the Pact Worlds and its planets maintain a "Pact Year" and a "Pact Day" as a standard form of temporal measurement, even if individual planets have days and years far longer than Pact Standard Time.

# **DAYS**

The names of the days of the week are simply numbered based on their position, as follows.

Firstday (Monday]

Seconday (Tuesday]

Thirday (Wednesday]

Fourthday (Thursday]

Fifthday (Friday)

Sixthday (Saturday]

Seventhday (Sunday]

# **MONTHS**

The names of the months of the year are traditional, a remnant from a time before the Gap. and are believed to date back to lost Golarion. They're named in honor of gods both current and ancient, as follows.

Abadius [January)

Calistril (February]

Pharast (March)

Gozran (April)

Desnus (May)

Sarenith (June)

Erastus (July)

Arodus [August]

Rova (September]

Lamashan [October]

Neth (November]

Kuthona (December)

# **BEYOND THE PACTWORLDS**

Outside the Pact Worlds, other planets and systems maintain their own calendars, timelines, and temporal measurements. Due to the nearness of Absalom Station everywhere in the galaxy, many societies have knowledge of Pact Standard Time and track it alongside their own.

for concepts that mortal minds have difficulty grasping as a physical description, for within the Great Beyond, anything is possible. For more information on other planes of existence, including the **Drift,** see page 182.

years before the Gap,a mysterious civilization called the First Onesutilized Aballon's abundant metals and solar energy to build a host of servitor machines. The First Ones tasked their machine progeny with harvesting the planet's resources-and then they left. Abandoned by their creators, the machines upgraded themselves over time and formed their own societies. The machines, now called anacites, have evolved into many forms and functions and established a utopian civilization where androids and other synthetic beings are welcomed as siblings. • •

**ABALLON \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_** As the closest world to the sun of the Pact System, AbaiIon is a warm ball of rock and precious metals. Its natural environment is a vast landscape of gray rock, replete with visible mineral veins and broken by the presence of impact craters and vast mountains of rock and iron. Little atmosphere envelops this planet, and beyond the protection of the immense megaplex settlements survival is difficult for any traditional organic lifeform. However, hidden atop and under the planets surface are precious metals sought by countless civilizations, the ice necessary for environmental engines which facilitate non-robotic life-forms on the world, and the ruins of the mysterious species known as the First Ones.

Throughout the Pact Worlds and beyond, AbaiIon is known as the planet of robots. With a massive artificial indigenous population known as anacites, the world aptly dubbed as

"the Forge'' is a refuge for countless other constructs, such as androids and the growing number of sentient robotic organisms (SROs).Since the judicial systems of some worlds remain partial to organic life-forms over constructs, many artificial species seek the utterly impartial legal systems of AbaiIon as a refuge and a place to start over. Those who have their crimes absolved on the world of robots often choose not to leave.

Aballon's history is inexorably tied to the mysterious species known as the First Ones. These enigmatic beings seeded AbaiIon with robotic life in the form of anacites in ages long ago and even before the Gap. Exactly what reasons the First Ones had for leaving the anacites behind remains one of the galaxy's many mysteries, but the recent discovery of more First Ones installations in the system's sun and orbiting the distant star of Sota means this ancient civilization clearly had (or still has) some grand design in mind.

Incredible ancient ruins are scattered around Aballon's surface, like relics from a completely different era-or one unbelievably beyond our current time. These cities

once belonged to the First Ones and have remained relatively abandoned since the species departed for places unknown. Close to holy sites in the programming of anacites, the local populace rarely permits intrusion into these realms, save for highly monitored and vetted exploration missions from groups like the Starfinder Society or major corporate interests.

Even today, Aballon's anacite population is divided on their belief in the First Ones. Until only recently, the planet's divisions came in the form of Those Who Wait and Those Who Become.

Anacites belonging to Those Who Wait believe that they must await the return of the First Ones and that their potentially limitless existence was intentional in providing the best forms to prepare for the First Ones' return. Conversely, Those Who Become have taken on the mantle of becoming the newest iteration of the First Ones, traveling beyond the realm of the Pact System and settling new worlds with new robotic life-forms in the tradition of their forebears. Only in recent years has a new division occurred within the strata of mechanized life: Those Who Are. This newest belief formed around the concept that life on Aballon should find its own path and not be shackled to the whims of a species who disappeared from the cosmos in a time long forgotten.

The robots of Aballon have created an ever-expanding mechanized utopia on their world. Vast settlements known as megaplexes unerringly spread across the planet s surface, replacing the rock with cyclopean cities of metal that cater to all forms of life. Some of these megaplexes, like the magitech wonder of Endeavor or the Horse Eye Orbital Plate, are notable

for their specific focuses or accomplishments throughout the Pact Worlds. However, the largest of these settlements is the planet's noted capital, the megaplex of Striving. Home to the leading Insight Array, the impartial Machine Court, as well as the Church of Triune, Striving is a vast settlement that nears the size of nations on some worlds.

Aballon's natural ecology isn't one that normally attracts outside interest save for one particularly unique phenomenon: the world's aptly titled Ice Wells. Containing the world's only source of water, these sites formed from vast impact craters that concealed liquid from the sun's rays and allowed for the formation of several frozen jungles. The Xenowardens faction maintains a strong interest in these sites, putting them routinely at odds with factions of anacites. The presence of the plantlike khizars from nearby Castrovel in these jungles has only complicated matters further.

# RECENT EVENTS

Shortly after the birth of the Newborn, a second cataclysm rocked the Pact Worlds: the arrival and crash of the

> so-called Wreck of the Returned on the red world of Akiton. While the mysterious starship crashed on another planet, the implications of the vessel's history

> > and intentions had the most impact on Aballon. Though factions attempt to piece together the full truth, it's clear that the wreck is a vessel of First Ones origin. Anacites belonging to both Those Who Become and Those Who Wait have already sent missions to Akiton in hopes of unearthing more information, but these factions also equally prepare on Aballon, with Those Who Wait making more overt claims to the veracity of their beliefs.

Further adding tension to Aballon's ongoing situation is the recent discovery of another First Ones ruin: the recently unearthed subterranean city of Recondite. Located under the Ice Well of Frozen Nelumbo, the presence of the plantlike khizars and their allies in the Xenowardens have created a sort of stalemate, with the anacites luckily more focused on the Wreck of the Returned than evicting the organic lifeforms from the recently discovered ruin.

With activity related to the First Ones at an all-time high, along with growing interstellar conflicts throughout Near Space, Aballon is now abuzz with activity. Factions of anacites and local corporations constantly expand their operations to maximize their production, whether it be new generation starships or vast lines of new weapons to be sold to the highest bidder. Adventurers and mercenaries are in high demand to either test out new equipment or to act as the vanguard for exploratory missions into First Ones cities, where they might find new technologies to employ in this uncertain age.

Castrovel is a pristine jungle planet where thriving metropolises coexist' ' , " nature. Magical gateways called aiudara connect Castrovel's cities, which are governed by a planetary federation of elven councils, formian queens, and lashunta magnates. Castrovel's unique infosphere is a web of psychically linked minds; those without psychic senses connect by bonding with tiny psychic creatures called shefis.

# **CASTROVEL \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

The Mid

**Gravity** normal: **Atmosphere**normal **Day**1 day; **Year**1/2 year \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Government** varies

**Capital** Qabarat (officially undecided)

**Population** lashuntas, formians. elves

**Languages** Castrovelian. Common,Elven

**Religions** Green Faith, Hylax, Yaraesa

Outsiders often see the lush planet of Castrovel as an "exotic" destination teeming with hot, humid jungles known for dramatic storms, gorgeous seaside sunsets, gleaming ancient cities built on millennia of history, and teeming with

dangerous megafauna. They aren't wrong. However, beyond its seemingly inhospitable environment, Castrovel has flourished in the modern age of advanced technology. Three species-the telepathic lashuntas, insectile formians, and long-lived elves-founded the most prominent civilizations to spread across the jungle world. Connected by magical portals, known as aiudora, the nations of Castrovel have banded together to form a powerful coalition that cements their role within the wider Pact Worlds.

Castrovel is roughly divided among four distinct continents. The largest continent. Asana,is the homeland of the telepathic lashunta species. It's a continent with well-charted borders, but its interior still contains haphazardly scanned locations or regions with foliage so dense only ground expeditions can penetrate. The continent known as the Colonies is home to the myriad formian hives that comprise the nation-equivalent of their civilization. Sovyrian is home to aiudaran elves and, until very recently, was an extremely isolationist region that

wasn't welcoming to non-elven outsiders. Finally, the vastly uncharted primeval continent of Ukulam is thought to be the most intriguing region of Castrovel to academics. This continent has seen little in the way of formal settlement from other powers, as it's home to the planet's most vicious weather patterns as well as its most ferocious megafauna.

When shirrens arrived in the Pact Worlds, one of their earliest achievements was beginning a process of brokering peacebetween the formians and lashuntas who had endlessly battled for resources and control of territory. The eventual cessation of hostilities was an outcome that few believed they would ever live to see, and even fewer of those who lived during the generations of war and isolation would comprehend the current state of a fledgling global republic between the world's three greatest powers.

The lashuntas of Asana have long been separated into powerful city-states, which form the basis of their influence on the planet to this very day. With time no longer spent focusing on formian incursions, the city-states have instead turned their attention to wider galactic affairs, with

new corporations expanding their influence and production beyond Castrovel's more immediate needs.

City-states have elected new < leaders in charge of implementing *2* technological and magical \* infrastructure across the planet, j\* These officials work closely with formian hive representatives.

Thelatest development on this I front has been the creation of an Wj ever-widening psychic infosphere \j across the world's continents, ■ save for Ukulam where installation o" psychic amplifiers is only recently being tested. This new form of network is open to traditional access via technological means but is intended for psychic species to take part in, a decision that has caused numerous psychically attuned species within the Pact Worlds (as well as several beyond) to take note and settle on Castrovel.

Anyone who isn'tpsychic can choose to bond with a shefi, a tiny creature with a fluid form and pleasing aura that's said to be an adorable companion as well as useful living hardware.

The many hives of the formians spread across the Colonies have come together in a similarly unified purpose, sending drones and workers out to other parts of the world to assist in vast endeavors of agriculture and architecture. While most remain committed to this unified world, there are whispers of some formian queens who seek to return to the old ways in hopes of expanding their respective territories. Luckily, recent news from Ukulam has somewhat quashed these visions of conquest but hasn't completely stopped some formian hives from conscripting adventurers and mercenaries for reconnaissance and defense.

Sovyrian is home to elves and a few incognito outsiders. The decadent nation is one of the oldest institutions within

the Pact Worlds. Ancient elves founded Sovyrian long before the Gap. Later Sovyrian scholars were some of the first to explore distant worlds. Following the Gap, the people of Sovyrian quickly cut themselves off from the remainder of the Pact System, entrenching themselves on their continent and using their vast magical knowledge to protect themselves from outside threats. Only major political events, such as the formation of the Pact, forced the ruling elven elite to acknowledge outsiders-grudgingly, lest they find themselves left in the dust by more progressive culture.

To preserve the delicate ecology of the planet, all of Castrovel's post-Gap industry was established on its single moon, Elindrae. The rocky moon had few resources and no popular significance, making it fertile ground for vast industrial enterprises and corporate land grabs.Factions often fought over territory on Elindrae. Lunar conflicts between lashuntas and formians were some of the last to die down in the peace talks between both species. Today, even the elves of isolationist Sovyrian maintain shipyards on the moon.

Shortly after the Gap an anonymous patron paid a vast

sum of credits to all three Castrovelian powers to secure a section of the moon that has since n been left untouched by industry. Though the most common rumor claims the

> Xenowardens made this bid in an attempt to contain the spread of industrialization, the truth is far more perplexing: the Church of | Yaraesa and a wealthy individual I from Absalom Station's Ring sector who refused to be named purchased it

# **— F RECENT EVENTS**

Along with rhe recent push toward a unified global republic, the greatest change sweeping Castrovel comes from the typically

reserved continent of Sovyrian and its elven population. A generation of post-Gap bom elves is coming to prominence and power, and with them a push for less isolationism and more unity with the other peoples on their home world and with the broader coalition of the Pact Worlds. Many of these typically younger elves have actively disobeyed tradition to partake in the development and deployment of Castrovel's psychic infosphere.

The rapid industrialization of Elindrae was intended to preserve Castrovel's biodiversity and maintain ecological balance for generations. But it wasn't a solution, simply a displacement. Instead, this endeavor has spawned one of the greatest threats to Castrovel's safety since the Gap. A congealed, semi-sentient polluted mass unleashed a massive attack on the moon before launching itself from space tower toward Castrovel.Though its exact landing point was difficult to discern, the polluted amalgam crashed deep in Ukulam. Since its arrival, warped and twisted formian-like creatures emerge from the jungle, and ravenous radioactive oozes consume Castrovel's most untamed wildland.

Floating in Lost Golarion's empty orbit, this bustling space station is the center of interstellar trade and governance among the Pact Worlds. Nobody knows who built Absalom Station because it appeared sometime during the Gap.The station's ancient magitech reactors are powered by the legendary *Starstone,* which also acts as the most powerful Drift beacon, inviting tourists and immigrants from faraway worlds. Hundreds of corporations and embassieshave built headquarters on the station, and a diverse population calls it home.

# **ABSALOM STATION \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

TheNexus

**Gravity**normal (artificial); **Atmosphere** varies (artificial) **Day 1** day (artificial): **Year**1 year \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Government**Prime Executive advised by a council (The Syndicsguild]

Population humans, many others

**Languages** Common,many others

**Religions All**

Spinning in space at the same location as the lost world of Golarion, the immense artificial satellite named Absalom

Station was built sometime during the Gap<sup>f</sup> but not much else is known about its construction or original purpose. As its secrets are lost to that broken time, Absalom Station is one of the galaxy's greatest mysteries, not only for its connection to the legendary lost Golarion, but because of its power source: the Starstone. The space station is the home of humanity [and other ancestries from Golarion) in the wider cosmos as well as the seat of power for the vast government that manages the Pact Worlds. Having endured since the time immediately after the Gap, the station acts as a bastion against the galaxy's many threats while also being a tempting invasion point for outside powers who covet the Starstone and its ability to redefine Drift travel.

Since the Gap removed all references of the station's past from recorded history, nobody agrees on the original intended purpose of Absalom Station. Instead, the most commonly held

opinion is that Absalom Station was a refuge built in the final days before Golarion vanished from the star system. Whether it was whisked away by the gods, destroyed by Rovagug breaking free from its prison, stolen by a jealous entity from another reality, or obliterated by a magical superweapon is the hot topic of countless theories. Nobody knows for sure.

While many humans had already migrated throughout the Pact System and beyond, Absalom Station became something of a new home for humanity among the stars, and in the decades following the Gap, many humans traveled to the immense station and established roots that last to this day, over three centuries later. Other ancestries from the missing world did the same, and old factions like the Hellknights clung to the hauntingly familiar location.

The Starstone that powers the station is one of the most highly sought-after artifacts in the cosmos. It's believed to be responsible for the ascension of several powerful deities, notably lomedae the patron deity of the Knights of Golarion. Since the Gaps aftermath, the Starstone has yet to display its deifying powers but has instead become the most powerful Drift Beacon in existence, allowing starships across the cosmos to find safe harbor at the station in only a matter

of days instead of the weeks or months that . ■ some journeys could take. This effect has \ V made the station a target. It drew the ire of its neighbors in one of the earliest assaults by the Eoxians [known as the Magefire Assault], followed by Veskarium aggression, and more recently the Azlanti Star Empire as the

new regime reconsiders how to handle relations with their long lost home system.

The Swarm seems to slowly advance toward Absalom Station, as though innately attracted by the Starstone's call.

Absalom Station is divided into a few major sections. The Eye is a vast transparent dome that contains lofty skyscrapers several hundreds of stories tall along with interlinking corridors and a few large roads, all of which are linked by interspersed parks and green areas.

The great circle of corridors and chambers extending out from the station is known as the Ring. It contains notable locations like the campus home of the Starfinder Society, the Lorespire Complex, in the district known as Drifter's End. This region is constantly abuzz with activity and is the most likely location for new arrivals or passersby to first touchdown on the station and get their footing in the galaxy's most notable hub.

Docks and hangars jut out from the Ring and form the region known as the Arms. Here, travelers and visitors dock vessels to depart or seek repairs.Several sections with custom atmospheric tastes, like Fogtown or the Puddles, cater to those who seek an alternative environment, such as underwater or gaseous terrain. Once used as quarantine sections during the

great Stardust Plague, many of the sections of the Arms have been converted into affordable housing.

The aptly titled Spike is the assemblageof magitech systems that keep Absalom Station running. The immense machine of the Starstone Engine, which encases the Starsfone itself, are all located in the Spike. Though the galaxy's potentially most important artifact rests here, the remainder of the Spike is left over to gang-controlled areas where small communities struggle to make ends meet and survive. Attempts to resolve the greater woes of the Spike have met with little success, as unlike much of the rest of the station, the population is largely transient.

A vast fleet [technically several fleets] of starships orbit Absalom Station,forming the unofficial section known as the Armada. Not every vessel that arrives from the Drift docks at the station, and instead many use the Pact System as a waypoint to other Pact Worlds or to systems in Near Space. Other such arrivals are simply too massive to effectively dock with the station and dispatch shuttles to carry crew onboard. Some organizations, most recently military vessels belonging

> to the Veskarium, are given wide berths and only allowed to dock via shuttles dispatched from Absalom Station.Lost within the Armada are several abandoned vessels, including a slew of starships that emerged from the Drift as husks. The Starfinder Society and several corporate interests routinely hire adventurers and salvagers to investigate these drifting ships, though there's rarely a shortage of such vessels and instead fewer willing to take the risk of exploring them.

# RECENT EVENTS

The destruction of the distant world of Aucturn unleashed psychic disturbances across the Pact Worlds, but few locations were in as much jeopardy as Absalom Station. The sudden appearance of the enigmatic midwives and their alien servants led to the station's malfunction, and many station inhabitants keenly remember where they were when the Newborn's cosmic birthday occurred. Since the deity's awakening, strange phenomena continue to be reported with alarming regularity, and groups like the Eyeswide Agency and Starfinder Society sometimes get called in to investigate.

Perhaps the most disturbing recent rumors of late, believed to be handed down from staff working on the Starstone Engine, are the murmurs of a supposed Starstone awakening. Those closest to the station's power source have begun complaining of strange dreams in their scant off-hours. These dreams seem replete with visions of a shattered landscape featuring faraway broken cities and a grand, yawning chasm that seems to encroach from the distance. Within the visions is a one-eyed dwarf sitting upon an ornate throne, accompanied by a throng of orc techno-barbarians. Exactly what these dreams portend, no one yet knows.

was once a booming industrial paradise overflowing with wealth from mining thasteron, a starship fuel that fell out of favor after the discovery of hyperspace. Now, Akiton is a decrepit battlefield for corporate interests, crime families, and survivalist communities.

The *Battlefield*

**Gravity** low; **Atmosphere** thin

**Day** 24 hours. 40 minutes: Year *2* years \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Government** council of city-states

**Capital Ari**

**Population** humans (hylki), ysoki, shohhads, contemplatives, ikeshtis, kasathas

**Languages** Akitonian, Common, Shobhad, Ysoki **Religions** Abadar, Devoured Lao Shu Po, Oras

The fourth planet from the sun, and once the closest planet to Lost Golarion, the red planet of Akiton is a dying world. Even in the Gaps aftermath, people looked at Akiton as a driver of economy with its critical fuel source for travel, the substance

**AKITON \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_** known as thasteron. All Akiton's standing and golden opulence turned to bitter ash with the advent of Drift travel. In the following three centuries, the red planet has become an ever expanding wasteland ruled by petty city-states and beholden to ruthless corporate interests.

> Iron-rich mineral deposits give Akiton its unofficial title of the "red planet" along with countless other mineral elements that have been mostly mined to depletion. A vast ice shelf along the planetary poles known as the Winterlands is a site of frozen liquid, though much of the usable water sources can only be found at depths of almost 100 feet, necessitating extraction machines, magic, or other conversion methods for locals to gather water.

> It's almost impossible to speak of Akiton's mineral wealth without talking about thasteron. This native substance gave the worlds of the Pact System a keen edge in pre-Drift travel as well as granted Akiton leverage against larger powers like those of CastroveL Eox, and Triaxus. When Drift travel removed its necessity, the ensuing thasteron bust destroyed Akiton's

once vibrant economy and condemned countless citizens to generational strife and poverty if they decided to remain on the red planet. Even foreign powers from other worlds gave Akiton a wide berth, as without thasteron, the planet held little value to outside powers. Only Akiton's large population and relative proximity to Absalom Station ensured a position within the powerhouses of the Pact Worlds.

Despite its Pact status, the red planet boasts no central global authority. What passes for government comes in the form of the planets remaining city-states, like the metropolises of Ari or Maro. Each city-state maintains its own unique selfcontained government, and each city-state is allowed to present an equal number of delegates to represent Akiton within the Pact Council. Beyond the fortified positions of these states, lawlessness is commonplace, with only the authority of powerful gangs or corporate-sponsored holdouts offering a semblance of order. Non-aerial travel between sites is a complicated matter, and one of the most common reasons for factions to hire extra muscle for protection. For some Akiton is a liberated paradise, and for others it's a lawless hell.

One of Akiton's most famous exports starship components, though not from a manufactured facility, but instead from salvaging of crashed starships. Vast graveyards of crashed starships, fl| known to be dated from sometime fl during the Gap. litter Akiton's fl vast open deserts of red sand, fl: These sites rarely yield more [ :I f. than scrap metal, but time and fl time again, enterprising salvagers fl have uncovered some previously fl£? unknown technology or an especially rare component still prized in modern starship construction. No crash is as important, however, as the recent impact site known as the Wreck of the Returned, which is purported to be the crash site of a vessel belonging to the mysterious First Ones. are

While most shun Akiton as a dwindling backwater within the greater worlds of the Pact, countless corporations have found means to exploit the red planet's woes to their benefits. Corporations as small as private contractors, all the way up to the gilded offices of AbadarCorp, operate across Akiton. Some larger corporations go as far as sponsoring so-called corporate towns, where an entire settlement is established with the resources and protection of a larger corporation to entice families to move in and take on jobs related to corporate needs, handling everything from terraforming the land to mining resources and engineering pleasant [but temporary] cities. On Akiton, such towns are typically used to sponsor mining operations, but others are built for weapons testing or experimental use of biotech and less ethical products.

Countless species call Akiton home. Most notable are the crimson-skinned humans who migrated to the red planet tens of thousands of years ago, taking on the ancestral name hylki. Hylki are human in most other respects except skin

tone and common lineage. Large populations of ysoki thrive on Akiton, and though they often receive some ridicule for their flighty nature in other cultures, Akitonians respect ysoki clans for their ingenuity. Contemplatives living in underground compounds have discarded much of their physical form for heightened mental capabilities, and many have moved on to explore or make homes on other worlds. Those who remain observe the inhabitants living above them as part of some grand experiment, or ignore them entirely in favor of other problems. Red-scaled ikeshtis and powerful shobhads honor their ancestral traditions while embracing modern technology and acquiring assets and allies outside their traditional lands.

Some kasathas have settled on Akiton or plan to colonize it. The original mission of the /dori was the colonization of Akiton, though it was terminated when the Doyenate discovered peoples already living there. The vast majority of kasathas remained aboard the /dori, but some extremists and troublemakers have left their worldship to try and settle on Akiton regardless. This influx of kasathas has come in two

forms: those who seek to live on the world and set down new roots in harmony with the locals, and those

who instead believe that the world is their birthright and form new factions outside the city-states to try and forcefully seize control for themselves and their families.

# **RECENT EVENTS**

One sudden change has come to Akiton's lack of fortune: the crashed starship now known as the Wreck of the Returned. This r immense vessel blasted into the Pact System, and before even the Stewards could react to its presence, it descent onto Akiton's surface. In the aftermath

of its arrival, countless organizations sent expeditions inside, only to find the mysterious technology of the First Ones employed within. Soon after, defensive measures activated and forced intruders out of the wreck, pushing them back with overwhelming firepower. Now, the wreck is a site of great interest to countless parties, including an ever-growing delegation from Aballon's anacite population of Those Who Wait. The Starfinder Society is another major player on Akiton sponsoring delves into the Wreck of the Returned.

An unexpected and little known change to Akiton's fortunes has come with recent financial investment from the Veskarium military. A hidden benefactor within the Veskarium has recently spent millions of credits toward testing a new generation of thasteron-based weapons of mass destruction. Though such weapons are only theoretical, and highly dependent on magical stimuli to activate, the Veskarium is eager to pay an exorbitant amount to obtain a working model to employ in its burgeoning war with the Azlanti Star Empire. Only time will tell if a second thasteron boom is in Akiton's future, but for now, Veskarium military research stations are popping up across the red planet.

Verces is a tidally locked world with a sun-scoured light side, a frozen dark side, and a belt of opulent cybercities linked by high-speed bullet trains. This band of megacities called the Ring of Nations is governed by an elected Grand Assembly; meanwhile, frontier outposts and bandit camps struggle to survive outside city borders.

The Line

**Gravity** normal: **Atmosphere** normal

**Day- Year** 3 years\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Government** grand assembly of 27 countries [Ring of Nations)

**Capital** varies by nation

**Population** verthanis, shirrens, humans, kasathas

**Languages** Vercite

Religions Lambatuin, Oras, Weydan

Regarded by many as the technological hub of the Pact Worlds, the tidally locked world of Verces has a long and storied history in space travel. Verthani aetherships were the first to risk the void between planets, accomplishing a

**VERCES \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_** miraculous deed only previously achieved through magic. Today, Verces and its Ring of Nations are an industrial and corporate powerhouse within the Pact System andNear Space, while the regions of Fullbright and Darkside are wastelands best left to foolhardy adventurers and brave explorers, or the bandits and outlaws that roam them.

> Verces lacks a normal day and night cycle. Because of the planet's locked orbit, the world has only a small sliver of land that's considered habitable, while the remainder of the planet is broken into the frigid black of Darkside or the glassed deserts of Fullbright. A contingent of winged humanoids originating from lost Golarion, strix, maintain a permanent presence in Fullbright as one of the few settled regions under the scorching sun. While smaller groups eke out a living in the frozen void of Darkside, the region's most notable inhabitants are the feral creatures known as bloodbrothers, resembling a mix between a millipede and a muscular humanoid, that grotesquely prey on creatures by using them as auxiliary hearts.

Theresidentverthanisareboththeworldsnativeinhabitants and its largest population. The species are on average eightfoot tall humanoids with large, protruding black eyes and skin that changes complexion to emit complex patterns. Verthanis also have an innate attraction to technology and augmentations; rarely does one find a verthani who hasn't received at least some augmentation in their lifetime, and many sport several as both a social display and a matter of preference. Along with these inhabitants, Verces also houses a large population of humans who emigrated before the Gap as well as clusters of kasathas and other species who settled in the deserts of Fullbright because the harsh climate was akin to their ancestral home world. A sizable contingent of shirrens reside on Verces, as the planet was the first to offer them sanctuary in their flight from the Swarm. Many still live in Darkside or Fullbright where their ancestors crash-landed while fleeing their pursuers. Today, the Shirren Colonies are some of the densest population centers on Verces.

Culturally, verthanis once organized their communities into a caste system, where individuals would choose at puberty to join one of three castes. The Augmented embraced magical and technological innovations andused them to enhance their physical forms. The God-Vessels became receptacles for divine energies, often becoming followers of one or more religious powers. Finally, the Pure Ones maintained physical purity and eschewed leaning too much into the path of the Augmented or God-Vessels, acting as intermediaries between the two castes. Though some traditional verthani family units and isolated communities maintain this practice, the choosing of castes has become more of a stylistic choice for younger generations, and even non-verthanis living on Verces have been admitted to desired castes, with the Augmented being a particularly appealing choice for many.

A coalition of 27 countries comprises Verces's central government, the Ring of Nations. Represented to the Pact by a democratically-elected Grand Assembly, the Ring of Nations originally formed the defense force known as the Stewards, which have since gone on to provide security and protection for the entirety of the Pact Worlds. The Ring of Nations is seen as an ideal government by many outsiders since Verces has not experienced any sort of civil warfare in countless millennia before the Gap.TheRing's outward stability deterred countless powers from ever challenging Verces, though in truth, many countries engage in hidden conflicts against one another that they painstakingly keep from public scrutiny to defend their world's appearance of total unity.

Fringe groups living outside the governance of the Grand Assembly struggle for survival along the terminal edge of Verces's ring zone and even into the Darkside and Fullbright regions. Known colloquially as the Outlaw Kingdoms, these

lawless or isolationist groups push for recognition by authorities within the wider Pact Worlds while receiving little more than scorn from the Ring of Nations. A growing group of foreign interests has pushed to grant the Outlaw Kingdoms a spot on the Pact Council, seeking to create further division between the representatives of the Grand Assembly and possibly opening a crack in the typically indestructible exterior unity of Verces's leading planetary government. Such a move could allow outside influences to establish a greater hold on the world, and certain powers eagerly seek to use the desperate groups of the Outlaw Kingdoms for their own ends.

Some firebrands in the Outlaw Kingdoms loudly oppose unification with the Grand Assembly, believing the future lies in forming their own representative government outside the influence of city-dwellers and foreign powers.

# RECENT EVENTS

The long era of peace and quiet economic growth continues on Verces. While there's little sign of the Grand Assembly undergoing any major shakeups, a relatively recent

> threat spreads across Verces: the rise of so called "techno-terrorism; or destructive events orchestrated by various factions using technology. Groups of techno

terrorists strike out against the GrandAssembly and foreign powers using new asynchronous methods. One such group, the Remakers, have begun abducting citizens and forcibly upgrading them with unwanted augmentations. These actions began by targeting those who sided with the God-Vesselsand Pure Ones castes and were believed to have been enacted by radical fringe elements of the Augmented. However, the recent influx of Veskarium refugees seeking to

escape the mounting war with the Azlanti Star Empire have found themselves the targets of Remaker abductions. A mounting number of missing persons have people fearing these techno-terrorists might be building a veritable army of abductees for some sinister purpose. They might even have ties to the Mechanizers.

On Darkside, the rare ice element known as shelynium has long been harvested for its apparent healing properties. However, shortly before the birth of the Newborn, excavation teams reported that known deposits of shelynium were suddenly evaporating. The rampant disappearance of this substance across Darkside has led to sudden glacial shifts, which have in turn disrupted the ecologies of many native creatures. Broods of vicious bloodbrothers, once entombed in icy stasis, rampage across the Darkside with a ravenous appetite. Attacks caused a decrease in the ice mining necessary to support the luxe lifestyle enjoyed in the Ring of Nations. The Grand Assembly is already planning to send military forces to contain the situation-an action that could incite further conflict with the Outlaw Kingdoms on Darkside!
