**ADVENTURES**

Factions in the galaxy are corporations, federations, and organizations united by mutual goals. Factions control vast resources and claim the loyalty of hundreds or thousands of people across the galaxy. Some factions wield political clout, claim historical significance, command armies, or gain power from ties to otherworldly beings.

The factions summarized here represent organizations with strong ties to the Pact Worlds that can also be found across the galaxy. Some of these institutions, such as the **Free Captains** and **Xenowardens.** represent groups that actively impact adventurers across the cosmos, whether it be contending with piracy or negotiating with planetary conservationists. Many factions work with interstellar governments, such as the **Knights of Golarion** and **Starfinder Society,** which both have deep ties to the Pact Worlds and stalwartly continue their quest to rediscover Lost Golarion. Other factions, like **AbadarCorp** and the **Hellknights** were founded within the Pact Worlds but have since expanded far beyond the borders of the Golarion System and into the wider cosmos.

Perhaps one of the most pervasive brand names in the Pact Worlds and Near Space, AbadarCorp is a mighty business conglomerate that produces almost any type of goods imaginable. It's the wealthiest corporation in the Pact Worlds and all of currently charted space, largely thanks to their standardized credit being accepted in almost every region [even parts of the typically hostile Azlanti Star Empire). They employ millions across the galaxy, either as AbadarCorp agents clad in their iconic armored suits or through numerous subsidiary corps.

While not every member of the corporation shares the company's religious beliefs, the highest-ranked executives and leadership all have strong faith in Abadar, the god of commerce. The current archdirector is the highest-ranking servant of Abadar in the Universe, meaning that there's little separation of church and corporation-most such distinctions only apply for taxation rules.

Outside the Golarion System, AbadarCorp's most notable endeavor is the establishment of several seed colonies throughout Near Space and the Vast. The company expends billions of credits to construct necessary components for these new settlements, often at reasonable prices with stipulations that AbadarCorp be provided finder fees or licensing arrangements for any discoveries made.

# FREE CAPTAINS

A loose association of pirates that first united in the Pact Worlds, the Free Captains have spread t throughout Near Space and even into the Vast. With humble beginnings in the Diaspora as a confederation of pirate

captains, the Free Captains have become the greatest threat to traders and tourists traveling the new Drift Lanes. Taking their name from a historical pirate armada on Lost Golarion, the Free Captains<sup>1</sup> fleets have pilfered and raided ships since the earliest days of Drift travel, and don't seem likely to stop anytime soon.

The recent creation of Drift Lanes created a brave new era for piracy. Now that stable shortcuts connect major trade hubs, Free Captain crews find it easier than ever to pillage and plunder. Many are fond of a new tactic that involves setting up ambush points on less-traveled portions of key trade routes. But as Free Captain crews grow more brazen, other organizations such as the Hellknights and Stewards are escalating their counter-piracy operations. Whether the Free Captains will rule the Drift like their namesakes ruled the high seas of old, or if Broken Rock will fall to a great police raid remains to be seen. ABADARCORP

# 0Q HELLKNIGHTS

Descended from the group of the same name from *r* Lost Golarion, the Hellknight orders are dedicated

to spreading throughout the stars, regardless of any casualties or perceived expense. Allied with fiendish forces from Hell itself, Hellknights offer their service for credits but also impose their specific brand of justice in situations that their leadership deems necessary.

Countless orders comprise the wider faction of Hellknights. The most prominent in the modern age is the Order of the Eclipse, which came to prominence as disenfranchised Hellknights united in the aftermath of the Gap. The Order of the Eclipse focuses its efforts on controlling messaging and dangerous media throughout the Pact Worlds. Hellknights of the Crucible constantly test themselves and scrutinize distant colonies and alien civilizations for brutal trials. Those who pass these tests receive impressive boons for their efforts, such as the bequeathment of *Helldrive* technology, which the Gideron Authority obtained from their ongoing relationship with the Crucible Hellknights. Civilizations that fail eventually face invasion by a more successful empire and their hired Hellknight mercenaries.

# KNIGHTS OF GOLARION

Following the Gap, a group of artisans, knights, and historians banded together on Absalom Station with the goal of discovering Lost Golarion's location.

Splitting into fleets, the self-proclaimed Knights of Golarion traveled the stars looking for their lost home world and performing deeds of kindness and valor along the way. Knights of Golarion are dedicated to preserving cultural touchstones from Lost Golarion while battling against forces spreading cruelty and destruction in remote parts of space. It was a fleet of Knights of Golarion who clashed with the Veskarium during the commencement of hostility between the Pact Worlds and the Veskarium, kicking off a wider conflict known as the Battle of Aledra. Later, the Knights valiantly protected Veskarium settlements when the vicious Swarm first invaded, and fought alongside their former foes to protect all of Near Space in the Swarm War. The Knights' shining cathedral ships can be found in almost any region of space. The core knighthood fleets protect the Pact Worlds and errantry fleets patrol far into the Vast. The Knights eagerly work alongside other organizations, including corporations like AbadarCorp and institutions like the Starfinder Society and the Stewards. Their most contentious foes include the Corpse Fleet, the Azlanti Star Empire, invading fiends, jinsul raiders, and the endless forces of the Swarm.

# STARFINDER SOCIETY

w The galaxy's most adventurous organization started as a tight-knit community that helped their neighbors survive the chaos on Absalom Station following the Gap. In 10 AG,the first members of the Starfinder Society Forum were elected and began funding expeditions. Since then, the Starfinder Society has been at the forefront of galactic exploration. Field agents form adventuring parties that take on missions into Near Space and the Vast to uncover the secrets of lost civilizations or discover magical and technological innovations from alien cultures. An elected First Seeker leads the Society, dedicating years of the Society's efforts to a specific goal, before stepping down and allowing a

new Starfinder to take the helm. Over the years, the Society has endured several notable setbacks. First, the Stardust Plague turned popular opinion against exploring distant space, as fears mounted about what diseases or malign forces could be unleashed. First Seeker Lanrah's tenure ended with her disastrous attempt to discover the truth behind the Gap, causing her death and a complete restructuring within the Society, plus a rule against future First Seekers from exploring the cause of the Gap. Later events like the Scoured Stars incident and Data Scourge decimated the Society's resources, but the tenacious organization always bounces back.

# XENOWARDENS

Xenowardens are druids, mystics, and scientists working together to preserve nature on a galactic scale. Their mission is protecting ecosystems all over the galaxy from industrialization and exploitation. The Xenowardens also work to repair ecological damage caused by interstellar incidents, such as reseeding worlds and regions devastated by conflicts like the Swarm War. Wildfire Xenowardens eradicate dangerous

invasive species and quarantine ecological contagions, often employing harsher methods than other branches.

Xenowardens create and nurture living starships created with secret primal magical rituals. Arkships are plantlike armed transports that bond with a Xenowarden crew. The Greenspeaker, leader of the Xenowardens, always bonds to *Equilibrium,* the largest of the arkships and the most impressive weapon in the faction's arsenal.

# OTHER FACTIONS

Beyond the major factions listed above, other smaller factions are active within the Pact Worlds and beyond.

The Akashic Society has learned how to astrally explore the mysterious Akashic Record, a

dimension that contains all knowledge from everywhere in the multiverse. Akashic Society members, called scholars or scribes, have no unified agenda. They might work to understand the Akashic Record, add their own experiences to its vast archives, or protect its premises from interlopers and spies. Akashic scholars and scribes often travel the multiverse and work with copies of themselves from other realities.

# Deep Cultures Institute

The university known as the Deep Cultures Institute (DCI) has made its mandate to study cultures inhabiting the interior of stars. Their recent discovery of civilizations within the Golarion System's own sun has led to a proverbial gold rush of missions into Near Space and the Vast to research what many once considered a joke or conspiracy theory. Private investors and corporate sponsors are flooding the DCI with proposals and resources, but the Institute lacks enough staff to handle all the requests for solar exploration in faraway systems. To keep up, DCI leadership started hiring adventurers willing to literally go find some stars!

### Eyesudde Agency

Styling themselves as a psychic detective agency, the Eyeswide Agency operates under a sleepless neon eye. They solicit investigation services, offering everything from background checks and detailed analysis to more esoteric options like mind reading, magical remote viewing, and even communicating with the recently departed.

### SkyFire Legion

The Skyfire Legion is a historic institution from the draconic world of Triaxus. Once upon a time, a

A conservation society originally from Castrovel, great legion of dragons, dragonkin, and their bonded partners protected their communities from the perils of land, sea, sky, and space. Over time the legion grew and split into independent mercenary companies. Some defend Triaxus while others take high-paying, heroic missions across the Universe. Skyfire Legion units deployed into the ongoing Azlanti-Veskarium conflict, and different companies received temporary contracts from the Marixah Republic in Near Space to shore up defenses against escalating aggression from the Gideron Authority.

# **RELIGION**

Many individuals in the universe pay homage to at least one deity, whether it be one associated directly with their species or a deity with even further reach in the galaxy. Some might worship a local pantheon of deities, such as the elves of Sovyrian, or instead venerate a distinct philosophy like those of the vesk battle saints. Remember that some deities receive worship on countless worlds, sometimes even taking on different identities when interacting with different planets.

.. » i

Anyone can worship a deity, but those who do so devoutly should take care to pursue the faith's edicts [behaviors the faith encourages] and avoid its anathemas [actions considered blasphemous]. Each deity has a short description, followed by their edicts and anathemas.

The deities described here are considered core deities because people commonly worship them in the Pact Worlds, or because they play an important role in the Starfinder setting. For example, Lissala isn't a popular deity in the Pact Worlds, but every child in New Thespera says their prayers to Lissala, the divine patron of their great empire. Other deities exist in the Universe, including some of the ancient gods of Lost Golarion and deities so alien the peoples of the Desna's Path galaxy aren't able to comprehend their existence. Jg, THE DEVOURER

# ABADAR

Abadar is the god of civilization who actively works to spread commerce to uninhabited worlds.

He encourages adherence to the law and promotes commerce and trade within civilization. Abadar's most notable institution is AbadarCorp (page 198], a corporate church that controls one of the most common universal currencies. Abadar is also called the Master of the First Vault; accordingly, the great bank run by his holy megacorporation is named the Golden Vault.

**Edicts** earn wealth through hard work, follow the rule of law, spread commerce and civilization to distant worlds

**Anathema** engage in banditry or piracy, steal, undermine a law-abiding court

# BESMARA

Besmara is worshipped by pirate crews in shipping lanes across the galaxy. She encourages her faithful to plunder and pillage but stay loyal to their crewmates and obey their captain. Besmara has no church and is famously fickle when it comes to answering her followers' prayers, but she delights in seeing mortals flying her divine skull and bones [when she notices them at all]. Besmara's favor is sure to bring strife and success in spades, and only

the heartiest blackhearts dare invoke the Pirate Queen. **Edicts** stay loyal to a worthy captain and crew, take what you want, traverse the stars

**Anathema** betray shipmates, forsake piracy, settle on a planet

# **| DGITIGS** DAMORITOSH

Damoritosh the Conqueror is the patron deity of the Veskarium and a god of war. He demands his followers fight their battles with bravery and determination, no matter the form their struggles might take. Generals on the front lines of the Azlanti-Veskarium conflict pray to Damoritosh, just as people living with chronic illness or fighting a quiet personal battle call out for his aid back on Vesk Prime. Damoritosh commands an army of saints in divine wars on the outskirts of the Universe, but his mortal worshippers often call on them as well. See page 203 for more about the Battle Saints of Damoritosh.

**Edicts** crush your enemies, fight worthy foes, seek victory **Anathema** abandon your duty, back down from conflict, reject surrender

**jStfT** The Star-Eater has no concept of worship. It's completely unaware of its own nascent following, or even that it exists among other deities. This being of pure primal hunger seeks the fruition of complete universal entropy. The Devourer wants only for everything to end, and riles mortals under its influence into committing destructive acts toward that purpose. The Devourer doesn't even care about its heralds, the terrible atrocities that occasionally spawn from the most heinous acts committed by the Devourer's cultists or are summoned to wreak havoc by nihilistic priests. If the Devourer wins, all is lost.

**Edicts** engage in acts of destruction, kill sentient beings, tear down civilizations

**Anathema** create new magic, life, or technology without a destructive purpose; repair an object that won't inflict greater destruction

# ELORITU

secrets of magic and metaphysics to his followers.

His symbol is a puzzle: a ring of six magical runes that are each used individually in magical practice by different cultures across the universe. Only four runes are known to the peoples of the Pact Worlds, but Eloritu's scholarly church hopes to discover the final two. The answer to Eloritu's riddle is referred to as the Hidden Truth, an epithet commonly used for the god himself.

**Edicts** master the magical arts, preserve knowledge, uncover secrets

**Anathema** destroy knowledge, embrace technology over magic, reveal important secrets

# HMLflX

Hylax was a kucharn hive queen who ascended to divinity long before kucharn mutated into the aggressive hive mind everyone else calls the Swarm. Shirrens rediscovered Hylax when they broke free from the hive mind generations later. Hylax reached out to her ravaged children, swaddling them in comfort and communion but never demanding they give up their free will or personhood. Shirrens began worshipping Hylax as the Forever Queen. Prophets joyfully predicted a future era when all hives, cities, and communities in the galaxy would coexist in harmony. Hylax's followers don't proselytize, believing that even other gods will join Hylax's holy utopia as egalitarian members, but they welcome believers of all ancestries to share their faith.

An archaic sect of Hylax's church on Nchak believes the Forever Queen's departed advisors, who were slain long ago by an apostate who blamed Hylax for the Swarm's endless assault on the galaxy, have reincarnated into "golden larvae" who must be mentored so they can help Hylax usher in an age of universal peace.

Edicts build relationships with others, welcome others into your community, work toward peace

**Anathema** betray your community, start an unnecessary conflict, turn your back on a loved one

multiverse through astrophysics and cosmology. Unlike most outer gods, Ibra pushes its worshippers toward enlightenment and connection rather than despair and

isolation. Most of Ibra's churches are cosmological research institutes, and many of its worshippers are solarians who embody cosmic forces using their powers or scientists who dedicate their lives to trying to understand existence.

**Edicts** embrace cosmic unity, learn about the cosmos, study science

Anathema fear the unknown, settle for easy answers, undermine the scientific process

# LflMBOTUIN

The Polyphonic Collective is a divine group who eagerly embrace new concepts and ideas, argue over everything, and quickly move on to the next hot topic. They're omnipresent in all infospheres. Lambatuin adores chatting with-and sometimes troll ing—their worshippers, and any anonymous chatter could be one of them, manifesting for just long enough to lead someone to a new idea, ridiculous joke, or lively conversation about an unusual topic.

Edicts explore infospheres, share opinions, try new things **Anathema** follow stagnant routines, isolate yourself socially, unplug from infospheres for too long

# LOO SHU PO

Lao Shu Po is an ancestral deity important to many ysoki due to her areas of concern encompassing

the protection of ratkind as well as thieves. Legend has it that she was once a simple rat who gnawed on the corpse of a dead god and became divine. Her selfish teachings seem at odds with ysoki cultural values of collaboration and generosity, but traditional ysoki families still leave offerings for Grandmother Rat or wear charms to attract her favor (and ward off her spite). A secretive guild of assassins in the Diaspora claims Lao Shu Po as their divine benefactor, while ysoki religious traditionalists deny their claims as blasphemy.

**Edicts** betray others before they betray you, survive at all costs, take what you need

**Anathema** harm a rat, risk too much for someone else, work honestly for something you could steal instead

# LISSflLfl

Lissala is an ancient deity from Lost Golarion who later became the patron goddess of the

Azlanti Star Empire. She's precise, rigid, and intolerant of disobedience, but she rewards those who follow her strict teachings. Known as the Scion of Seven, she empowers the Azlanti state church with unholy magic. It's said that only her chief priest can look upon her remade form, imagined to be a colossal winged mech with seven unique configurations, including a serpentine form with six wings, a humanoid torso, and a seven-pointed star for a head.

**Edicts** know your place, serve a greater cause, work hard and demand others do so as well

**Anathema** disobey a superior, settle for easy rewards, shirk your duties **THE VAST** IBRfl

# Ibra the Inscrutable is an alien god exploring the

# MEMEL

Meyel is an ancestral deity worshipped by pahtras from Pulonis. She empowers her followers

to fight against oppression, create art, and live free. She prefers creation to destruction but never backs down from a righteous battle, earning her other name: The Dancing Fury. Meyel chooses a few pahtras in every generation to bless with her holy luck. Once the chosen has accomplished their divine task-creating a masterpiece, inspiring a revolution, or saving another person's life- Meyel's spark passes on to another pahtra who needs a bit of luck.

**Edicts** delight in movement, express yourself, fight for freedom **Anathema** bow to oppressors, devalue the arts, stifle personal expression

# THE NEWBORN

The Newborn is an outer god that hatched from the world of Aucturn, destroying the planet in the

process. As the horrific joy of its birth reverberates throughout the galaxy, devotees look to Aucturn's Child as the herald of a new cosmic era. But the Newborn itself might not even be aware it's being worshipped, or that it's been born at all, and only time will tell what monster or miracle this enigmatic new entity might become.

# ORflS

Oras is a god of transformation through science who evolved from a single-celled organism into

a deity. Notated as the Agent of Change by the biohackers and scientists who follow the path, Oras teaches others to unlock their true potential by surpassing their genetic code and augmenting their bodies.Oras encourages adaptation and transformation, attracting astrazoans, barathus, and other shapechangers to the faith.

**Edicts** adapt to new situations, embrace change, evolve **Anathema** fear change,prevent others from changing into their desiredforms, stagnateinto complacency

# PHflRflSMfl

Ancient and powerful beyond even most other gods,Pharasma judges the souls of all who perish from her throne in the Boneyard. Through these judgments, the Lady of Graves ensures that the natural cycle of birth and death-the River of Souls-isn't disrupted. The acceptance of undead in the galaxy, especially their protected status in the Pact Worlds, is seen by some extreme Pharasmin sects as an attack on the faith and the Boneyard itself.

**Edicts**destroy undead, lay bodies to rest, strive to understand ancient prophecies

**Anathema** create undead, desecrate a corpse, rob a tomb

Talavet the Storyteller is an ancestral deity worshipped on Kasath. Her faith traveled to the Pact Worlds aboard the /don. Talavet embodies celebration, home life, tradition, and living stories. Many kasathas partake in the elaborate ritual of piecing together a puzzleblade every morning and taking it apart every night, a sacred ceremony performed for Talavet and the memory of one's ancestors. Talavets faith is most prominent on the Idori and old Kasath, but friends of kasathan families sometimes adopt her rituals or join in storytelling around the hearth, which is an activity as holy to Talavet as attending church service.

**Edicts**follow traditions,preservehistory, serveyour community **Anathema** abandon traditions, betray your community, forget the past

) Triune is a fusion of three technological deities: Brigh the Precursor, Casandalee the Created,

and Epoch the Transcendent. Triune promotes technology and ethical creation of artificial life, and its Signal made Drift travel possible for many societies. Some of Triune's followers believe the All-Code is only the first phase in the merging of all gods in the Universe into a communal entity that will in turn join with all beings in existence.

**Edicts** advance the development of artificial life and artificial the siblings represent the concepts of overcoming suffering

intelligence, innovate new technology, promote Drift travel **Anathema** destroy a Drift beacon, subjugate artificial lifeforms, treat artificial life as inferior to organic life

# URGflTHOO

Urgathoa escaped the clutches of death and the afterlife, becoming the very first undead.She now

revels in the sharing of her hedonistic ways and spreading undeath. Eox is especially precious to Urgathoa, and even the grave world's most fearsome bone sages aren't too proud to bow before the Pallid Princess. Urgathoa sometimes allies with others who share hervision for the Universe, such as the CorpseFleet and the insatiable Eclipse, but finds their singleminded pursuit of a goal stifling.

**Edicts** become undead upon death, create or protect the undead, sate your appetites

**Anathema** your life deny your appetites, destroy undead, sacrifice

# WEMDON

Weydan crafts avatars of himself of all ancestries and genders to wander the galaxy and experience mortality-thus, he teaches his followers to treat anyone they meet with the same respect and dignity they'd show him. Weydan enjoys exploration and discovery, and he believes everyone deserves the equalright to pursue such experiences. His worshippers call him the Endless Horizon to honor his boundless nature and love for discovery.

**Edicts**defend the freedom and equality of all peoples, explore the unknown, seek new discoveries

**Anathema** aid systems of oppression, restrict the benefits of your discoveries to a privileged few, subjugate a sapient creature — TALAVET

# VflROESO

<sup>J</sup> spiritual practices that allowed her to attain divinity and cosmic enlightenment. As the Lady of Wisdom, Yaraesa unites the concepts of science and spirituality, pushing her followers to perfect themselves and transcend their natural potential. Yaraesa's worship is most common on Castrovel, where her mortal form once dwelled. Hints from ancient meditation litanies suggest that Yaraesa might have gained access to the Akashic Record before her ascension, leading to a resurgence of interest in her from new age communities and Akashic Society scholars.

**Edicts** expand the boundaries of scholarship and science, make learning and teaching a lifelong practice, strive for perfection

**Anathema** destroy knowledge, disparage the wisdom of other cultures and generations, take credit for another's intellectual labor

# /X ZON-SHELVN

Zon-Shelyn is the divine reunion of Shelyn and her estranged brother Zon-Kuthon. Together,

through art. strange beauty, and reunion with lost loved ones. Both gods still exist independently of one another but always appear together in their fused form whenever they're called upon as the Embrace or the Midnight Rose.

Edicts channel your pain into art, express yourself creatively according to your own aesthetics, seek beauty in dark places

Anathema conform to society's beauty standards, destroy or copy another's creative works, suffer in silence

# I **FAITHS AND PHILOSOPHIES**

Faith can express itself in more ways than venerating a single deity—or any deity at all A few examples of popular non deific religions and philosophies are presented below. These faiths and philosophies don't have an external godhead that offers benefits to devotees.

While most concede that the gods exist, some ■ ■ don't view them as worthy of worship, and a few even deny their presence altogether.

Edicts pursue your own agenda with faith in your ability to excel

Anathema offer prayers to a deity

# BATTLE SAINTS

An army of saints execute missions when Damoritosh commands it. Each saint has unique

powers with which to bless mortal believers. Many followers of Damoritosh pray to the saints for help in specific endeavors, and some vesk worship them as an alternative to their ancestral god. Devotees of each saint have their own beliefs and traditions, but they share the same general edicts and anathema.

Edicts battle impossible odds, honor fallen companions Anathema fight alone when companions could help, give up, show weakness to enemies SINBULARITISM

# THE CVCLE

Disciples of the Cycle follow a philosophy teaching that all aspects of existence follow the

endless cosmic cycle of stellar birth, death, and rebirth. They believe that the universe was and will be destroyed and remade by these cosmic forces, and that this ever-changing duality connects everything in the universe. Many solarians follow this philosophy, learning to wield these creative and destructive forces in battle through meditation and training.

Solarians study at cosmonasteries, secluded temples built around zero-gravity gardens. Most cosmonasteries are named for the nearest cosmic body or a quirk of the surrounding region of space, such as the Cosmonastery of the Empty Drbit on Absalom Station, named for the space left by Lost Golarion. Cosmonastery solarians dedicate themselves to performing stellar research while embodying the Cycle.

Edicts cultivate your own cosmic spark, embrace vitality and void as one, meditate on the nature of the Cycle

Anathema create imbalance in a system, participate in the unnatural destruction of a stellar body, resist natural cycles

# BREEN FAITH

Many Xenowardens and other mystics practice a modern version of the old Green Faith.

They view nature as sacred and draw strength from the knowledge of their place in the natural order. Many devotees of the Green Faith see themselves as protectors of a planet's ecosystem, and quite a few scientists find purpose and meaning in this philosophy as well.

Edicts guide civilization to grow in harmony with nature, preserve areas of natural wilderness, protect endangered species

Anathema allow abuse of natural resources, cause damage to natural settings, kill animals for reasons other than self-defense or sustenance IWI flTHEISM

The mysterious Prophecies of Kalistrade date back to older eras of Lost Golarion. The human known as Kalistrade created an economic mystic teaching that drew upon dreams he received. Today, modern prophets amass vast sums of wealth that they use alongside strict bodily guidelines to prepare themselves for a supposed eternal afterlife.

Edicts accumulate personal wealth, foster and aid mercantile pursuits, seek enlightenment through purity of self, welcome newcomers regardless of gender or ancestry

Anathema give charity to others; offer money to those who don't deserve wealth: overindulge in physical pleasures, food, or drink; spend money frivolously

Proponents of Singularitism believe that organic life will eventually become interconnected in a vast technological network, which shall then absorb all other forms of divinity, replacing the gods and primal forces of the Universe.

Edicts develop technology that improves communication Anathema interfere with technological advancement

# SONB OF SILENCE

The Song of Silence is a modern remix of the ancient Whispering Way that once spread on Lost Golarion. It teaches that Pharasma's judgment is a revolving door leading to an eternal cycle of suffering, and the only way out is undeath. The Song of Silence is prevalent on Eox, where dying mortals beg undead gurus for a surefire shot at immortality.

Edicts don't die before the ritual, spread undeath Anathema destroy undead creatures, worship Pharasma

# **CHAPTER 4: SUBSYSTEMS INTRODUCTION**

When **GAME** your game veers into uncharted territory or you want to emphasize an element of gameplay that usually gets overlooked or condensed into a single check, you can use a subsystem. As the name **BUILDING** implies, subsystems are extensions of the main rules system that allow you to explore a particular **THE PACT WORLDS** topic or style of play at your table.

Subsystems are a great way to add depth to aspects of your game that don't occur in combat but still have high stakes.This chapter begins with Victory Points, a structure that underlies much of the chapter, to help you build your own subsystems. Next are some of the most common subsystems you might need in your game, with advice on how to use and modify them. This chapter is organized into the following sections.

- **Victory Points** {page 206) provides a framework with which to build your own subsystems, detailing the fundamental structure that Starfinder uses for its subsystems.
- **Influence** [page 209) gives rules for more in-depth social encounters involving influencing NPCs.
- **Research** (page 212) shows you how to build an interesting structure for scenes where PCs research topics and seek information.
- **Chases** (page 214) are designed to represent the fastpaced feel of movie chase scenes.
- **Infiltration** (page 218) allows you to build infiltrations and heists where careful planning helps the PCs maintain an edge against their adversaries and pull off incredible capers.
- **Hacking** (page 222) provides a narrative challenge that represents accessinga secured computer.
- **CinematicStarshipCombat**(page 226) provides rules for cinematic and fast-paced starship combat that focuses on narrative interactions between the fighting starships.
- **Vehicles**(page 234) introduces aerial, terrestrial,aquatic, and space vehicles.

#### ■ **DECIDING TO USE A SUBSYSTEM**

When you have an exciting subsystem available, it can be tempting to use it anytime it can possibly come up [for instance, replacing every social scene with the influence subsystem). However, subsystems are most effective when used with intention. They're best when used for a component of the game that's meant to be at least a significant portion of a single session,when you want a different style of play than normal. Youshould avoid using a particular subsystem if many members of your group don't like it, or if use of a subsystem during play devolves into the PCsmaking a series of rolls that

# **INFLUENCE COMBINING SUBSYSTEMS**

Some of the subsystems in this chapter could interact in interesting ways when combined. For example, you could usethe chase subsystem during part of a shortcut characters take while piloting vehicles. You could use the infiltration subsystem to reach the first obstacle in the hacking or research subsystem. Ultimately, it's up to you to decide how two combined subsystems should interact to tell your group's particular story, though it's generally good to have a backdrop subsystem that you're tracking on a longer term to which the shorterterm subsystem contributes.

don't contribute to telling an interesting story. You should also be careful to ensure that whatever subsystem you use doesn't predominantly favor a specific player character or class since this can result in one person dominating the scene while the other players have nothing useful to do. Taking note of each PC's skills in advance can help make sure that each player has at least one opportunity to shine and help inspire certain elements of your subsystems by offering uses for skills that don't see as much use in your campaign, especially obscure Lore skills.

It's important to leave enough time and mental energy to make the subsystem feel special and to bring all the components and elements of the subsystem to life in the game world. Subsystems often require strategic thinking for players to succeed but also require roleplaying to glue together in a satisfying way. When presented with a specific structure on how to complete a challenge or obstacle, it can be natural for players to focus on that structure rather than the story that the mechanics are trying to tell. A subsystem stripped of ail its life and narrative depth can become nothing more than a large number of die rolls, however, and the last thing you want is to lose the magic, especially with a subsystem the PCs enjoy. Sometimes, a simple check is the right way to handle the scene,and that's okayl The subsystems will be there when you need them to spice up an adventure or really dive deep into a particular element or scene.

**GLOSSARY &**

Establishing a standard for when your players successfully overcome a complex obstacle ensures that the consequences of the challenge are meaningful and rewarding. Victory Points are a valuable tool to help build your own subsystems for tracking these successes.Many other subsystems throughout this chapter use these as well, though often by a different name.

Victory Points (or VP] allow you to track the PCs' progress using a subsystem to go beyond the results of a single check. Victory Points have almost unlimited potential as a campaign tool: you could track and resolve them within a single encounter, or you could collect them over the course of an entire campaign to determine the outcome of a particular story element.

# NAMING YOUR VICTORY POINTS

Selecting an evocative name for your Victory Points can better reflect the subsystem they track. The term "Victory Points" is unspecific, so you can create a name for your Victory Points that fits the theme of your adventure and helps the players feel more like they're taking part in the type of activity your subsystem represents. Examples of renamed VPs include Awareness Points (page 219], Influence Points (page 209], and ResearchPoints (page 212].The name should be representative, and the PCs should understand what it refers to. You can leave off the word "Points" if you prefer, though this section often retains it while describing how Victory Points can be used.

# VICTORY POINT SUBSYSTEM STRUCTURES

There are a few common structures for tracking Victory Points that you might use for your new subsystem. Youcould come up with a structure based off one of the subsystems below, or you could create your own completely different structure if none of them match the way you're running your game.The most important thing is to consider how the PCs or their opposition gain or lose various forms of Victory Points.

# ACCUMULATING VICTORS POINTS

The most common structure is to accumulate Victory Points toward a total, either stopping after reaching a success threshold or collecting as many Victory Points as possible in a given time frame and then measuring the results against various thresholds. The influence subsystem on page 209 is a great example of this structure in action: each PC has a certain number of chances to influence various NPCs, and after reaching a particular threshold of Victory Points with an NPC, the PCs have convinced that NPC.

In a variation of this structure, the PCs<sup>1</sup> adversaries can also accumulate Victory Points, giving the PCs a moving target—either to reach the goal before the adversary or to have more Victory Points than the adversary at the end of a given time frame. This is a great structure for you to use in a situation where the PCs face opposition rather than having the PCs accumulate Victory Points while adversaries decrease the total since it's dynamic and less at risk of resulting in a stalemate.

You can track a subsystem at a larger scale, like over the course of an adventure or campaign, by granting the PCs Victory Points for achieving difficult goals or making particular decisions. Such subsystems usually ask the PCs to compare their accumulated Victory Points against several ranked tiers that each have varying results on the story. Typically these results become more positive for the PCs as they acquire more Victory Points, but sometimes succeeding too fully could have unintended consequences, like convincing a mining colony to support work reforms only for it to escalate to an armed revolution. If youTe making your own subsystem, you might not define these ranks in full, but just use your best guess at the end.

### Accumulating Rolls

In cases where the PCs need to make checks to gain Victory Points, the amount they get for the degrees of success is up to you. The default scale detailed below works in most cases.

**Critical Success** The PCs gain 2 Victory Points.

**Success** The PCs gain 1 Victory Point.

**Critical Failure** The PCs lose 1 Victory Point.

This means that the result of a PC's check usually results in the party gaining either 1 or no Victory Points. However, specialized PCs have a solid chance of earning the party 2 Victory Points, and hare-brained schemes have a fair chance of losing the PCs1 Victory Point.

## DIMINISHING VICTORS POINTS

Using this method, the PCs start with a certain number of Victory Points, and rather than accumulating them, they attempt to avoid losing them. Perhaps the PCs are trying to prevent a hacker from getting into a database, keep an evacuation center safe during a hurricane, preserve a celebrity's untarnished reputation despite their troublemaking behavior, or are otherwise attempting to minimize damage, loss, or danger. This variant is less

common, but it's great at conveying the urgency of a situation as the PCs lose points. Sometimes it's necessary to add that sense of tension with this subsystem! Typically, when the PCs lose all their Victory Points, a negative event occurs. If they're on a timer, the final results might be better the more points they manage to keep before the

### Diminishing Rolls

time runs out.

Using this structure, the PCs typically lose Victory Points as a result of failed checks, though they can still lose them for making particularly poor decisions or behaving recklessly. Once again, you can use any scaling consequences that make sense, but the default degrees of success are as follows.

**Critical Success** If regaining ground is possible, the PCs gain 1 Victory Point. Otherwise, as success.

Success The PCs avoid losing any Victory Points.

**Failure** The PCs lose 1 Victory Point.

**Critical Failure The** PCs lose 2 Victory Points.

# MULTIPLE POINT SUBSYSTEMS

In a multiple point subsystem, you have more than one point system, each measuring something different For example, in a virtual racing vidgame, the PCs and their opponents both try to gain their own Lap Points, and whoever gets to 10

points first wins! Infiltration on page 218 offers a different example of a Victory Point subsystem with multiple types of points.

PCs try to get a certain number of Infiltration Points to successfully infiltrate a location while avoiding giving Awareness Points to their enemies through failure.

Consider combining the multiple points with a time factor, like in infiltrations, where the PCs automatically accrue Awareness Points over time at a slow rate.

# | OBSTACLES AND DCS

When preparing your subsystem, think of the obstacles PCs might face or avenues they can exploit when engaging in your subsystem. Set some DCs for them in advance, using the normal system for setting DCs. Everything else, you can improvise on the spot. If you think your DCs will be higher overall, when you set the number of points needed, choose a value on the lower end [see Setting your Scale below).

Think of some possibilities that are much easier and some that are harder. Who are your PCs opposing, and what weak points might that opposition have that the PCs could exploit? Set those DCs lower or make overcoming them grant more VP. PCs who do their research or come up with clever strategies should find it easier to overcome the challenge.

# | SETTING YOUR SCALE

The number of points it takes to reach a goal will greatly affect how your subsystem feels during play. If you want the subsystem to be used for a single scene, such as one

# **ENEMY VICTORY POINTS**

In addition to giving both the PCs and enemies Victory Points, as mentioned under Multiple Point Subsystems, sometimes it makes more sense to have only the enemies gain or lose Victory Points instead of the PCs. Even though the NPCsare also taking actions, it's usually best to increase or reduce the enemy's Victory Points based on just the PCs' actions since it maximizes the feeling of player agency. In some rare cases where the foes act directly against the PCs,you might have both PC and foe actions increase the foe's Victory Points.

Implementing such a subsystem might mean flipping the normal rolls. For instance, if the PCs were trying to lower their enemies' Influence over a faction, a critical success by a PC would lower the Influence by 2, a success would lower the Influence by 1, and a critical failure might give the enemies something to exploit, raising their Influence by 1. This uses the same effects as an accumulating roll, but 'damages" the enemies' VP instead of gaining VP for the PCs' side. While this is very similar mechanically to the PCs gaining VP,the thematic connection is much stronger for an intrigue-based story.

negotiation with a powerful NPC, set the number lower than if it's meant to take up most or all of a game session. The Victory Point Scales table below suggests possible values for your Victory Point scale. The "adventure-wide" scale is for subsystems that are part of a larger narrative, granting Victory Points when the PCs overcome entire encounters ordungeons, rather than as an encounter unfolds.

This larger scale is intended for subsystems that take a lot of the party's focus. A subsystem that runs in the background during an adventure should use a smaller scale. This is usually the "adventure-wide, sideline" value. It could be even lower, such as if you have a city-based adventure including several opportunities to interact with a street gang to get some small benefits. Though they appear throughout the adventure, you would use a lower value because attaining the VP is a minor part of the story. In fact, you might choose not to use a VP subsystem at all.

The table also lists numbers for one or more thresholds. These are the point values at which the PCs get a partial benefit [or, for a diminishing subsystem, take a drawback). You should grant partial benefits when the PCs reach a certain threshold or introduce twists to the subsystem to ensure they continue to feel engaged and rewarded over time.

# **VICTORY POINT SCALES**

| Duration<br>of Challenge | VP<br>End<br>Point | VP<br>Thresholds |
|--------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Quick encounter          | 3-5                | -                |

| Duration<br>of Challenge     | VP<br>End<br>Point | VP<br>Thresholds |
|------------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Long<br>encounter            | 7-10               | 4                |
| Most of a<br>session         | 15-25              | 5,<br>10,<br>15  |
| Adventure-wide,<br>sideline  | 15-20              | 5,<br>10,<br>15  |
| Adventure-wide,<br>forefront | 25-50              | 10. 20, 30, 40   |

The values also depend on various factors. These might include the DCs, the number of chances the PCs get to gain Victory Points, and the flexibility of how the PCs can deploy themselves [for example, if PCs are all forced to try something they might not be trained in, it could cause critical failures). They might also include the amount of effort the PCs need to spend on tasks that don't directly earn Victory Points-such as checks to Discover information about NPCs using the influence subsystem. Keep all these in mind when deciding what end point you want to use.

# I **RUNNING YOUR SUBSYSTEM**

When running your new subsystem, be sure to keep the challenges fresh by using a variety of different skills and options to encourage creativity and cooperation, rather than just using the same check over and over again, where PCs can expect diminishing returns. You can also use timers to encourage each PC to participate or even create mechanics that directly encourage each PC to participate, like setting penalties for the same PC attempting checks repeatedly, or for two PCs attempting the same check.

You can even have challenges that require all the PCs to participate. For instance, if a ritzy nightclub's owner is welcoming every guest individually, each PC might have to make an impression in their own way, or during infiltration, each PC might have to test their ability to Impersonate or Sneak. You'll likely find that some approaches should be automatic successes if the character making an attempt is well-suited to the task, or automatic failures for ideas that are likely impossible or that rely on abilities the character doesn't possess.

# **| REWARDS**

How you structure rewards for your subsystem depends greatly on its scope. A subsystem resolved in a single sitting usually gives accomplishment XP unless it's particularly demanding, in which case it could be considered a fullscale encounter. Meanwhile, subsystems that span over the course of multiple sessions or the entire campaign might generate accomplishment XP at meaningful milestones along the way. If you have a long-spanning subsystem that is fairly low profile and behind the scenes or isn't success-oriented, such as a subsystem to track what type of fan base the PCs' touring aggrosynth band will end up attracting based on the PCs' decisions, you might not give XP directly from the subsystem since, in that case, "success" is undefined.

Sometimes a single skill check isn't enough for the PCs to befriend or gain leverage over an NPC to achieve their goals. After all, even the sultriest idol can't make anyone a superfan with a single performance. In these cases, you can engage in a socialencounter using the influence subsystem

Influence is a short-term subsystem wherein the PCs accumulate Influence Points during a social encounter with an NPC to represent their increasing influence. These encounters are a race against the clock to reach Influence Point thresholds in order to sway the NPC. It's perfect for a single social gathering-whether it's a party, a boardroom meeting, or even an attempt to persuade a panel of judges during a game show.

Because of the variety of Influence skill options and the ability to use Perception to uncover more information, every character has something important to contribute in the influence subsystem, as opposed to situations where only one character has Diplomacy.

The influence subsystem divides a social encounter into rounds, with the number of rounds representing the length of the social event. Rounds last any amount of time that you determine, depending on the needs of the narrative, though somewhere between 15 minutes and an hour is typical.

During each round of an influence encounter, each PC can act once to either Influence or Discover.

#### **CONCENTRATE I LINGUISTIC**

You attempt to make a favorable impression on an NPCto convince the NPC to support your cause.

**INFLUENCE**

Choose an NPC, and attempt a skill check to impress that NPC.TheDC.and whether success is possible, depend on the NPC's preferences [typically found in the NPC's influence stat block).

**Critical Success** You gain 2 Influence Points with the chosen NPC.

**Success**You gain 1 1nfluence Point with the chosen NPC.

Failure You gain no Influence Points with the chosen NPC.

**Critical Failure** You lose 1 Influence Point with the chosen NPC.

# **DISCOVER**

#### **CONCENTRATE**

You watch, research, or study an NPC to learn more about that NPC's preferences. Choose an NPC and attempt a Perception check or an appropriate skill check determined by the GM. The DC is typically found in the NPC's influence stat block.

**Critical Success** Choose two of the options detailed in Success below: you can choose the same option twice to learn two pieces of information from the same category.

**Success** Choose one of the following: you learn which skill that can Influence the NPC has the lowest DC [skipping any skills that you already know), one of the NPC's personal biases, one of the NPC's resistances, or one of the NPC's weaknesses.

Failure You learn no information.

**Critical Failure** Choose a piece of information to learn about, as success, but the information is incorrect. For instance, you might think the NPC is susceptible to flattery when actually the NPC is resistant to flattery.

# **INFLUENCE STAT BLOEKS**

NPCs in the influence subsystem have little need for many of the statistics you'll find in an ordinary creature stat block. However, it

might help you to prepare for the social encounter by creating an influence stat block for each prominent NPC. These are optional; if you can keep most information straight in your head, you might skip this step or just write down the first three categories to keep the numbers straight.

Influence stat blocks are flexible and contain only the stats that are essential to running the NPC during a social encounter, leaving the rest out. The main stats that matter are the NPC'sPerception and Will modifiers.

#### **NPC NAME**

A succinct description of the NPC, such as 'awardwinning pop diva" or "famous ace pilot."

**Perception** The NPC's Perception modifier, plus potentially relevant abilities such as scent or fruesight.

**Will** The NPCs Will modifier, plus any special adjustments.

**Discovery** The Perception DC to Discover information about the NPC, as well as any skill checks to Discover their DCs.

# **SAMPLE STAT BLOCK**

In this example, the PCs try to convince a retired mechanic to help fix their starship that has crash-landed near a remote settlement in the Vast. It's a 3rd-level challenge. He's a busy, grouchy man who gives the PCs only 45 minutes (3 rounds] to make their case.

# **GROOVELOCK**

**LEVEL 3**

**| UNIQUE | SMALL | HUMANOID | YSOKI |**

Grumpy retired engineer

**Perception +9**

**Will** +12

**Discovery** DC 18 Perception, DC 15 Piloting, DC 16 Society

**Influence Skills** DC 16 Crafting (helping him Repair items around the settlement], DC 16 Junker Lore, DC 20 Performance (promising to perform for the settlement], DC 20 Piloting (recalling your experiences with your ship], DC 22 Diplomacy, DC 24 Deception

**Influence 4** Groovelock gives the party access to the settlements junkyard to fix their own starship.

**Influence 6** Groovelock introduces the party to his ysoki assistant. Nakwik, who helps them patch the starship up enough to reach a major settlement on the other side of the planet for full repairs.

**Influence 8** Groovelock and Nakwik help repair the starship and any damaged gear the PCs have whiie offering them a place to stay. \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Resistances** Groovelock regrets working with the same corporations that turned his settlement into a junk heap to the extent that he refuses to even use credits. Appeals promising payment in credits or discussing potential business prospects increase the check's DC by 2.

**Weaknesses** Groovelock values the craftsmanship of existing goods and restoring components others have cast aside as junk, and discussing repairing existing parts or using salvage to repair the ship reduces the Crafting DC by 2. \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Background** Groovelock was an engineer who helped broker a deal to expand the local recycling plant, but the contract allowed a careless megacorp to dump more waste than the small settlement could feasibly process. Blaming himself for his community's blight, the ysoki has dedicated himself to helping improve the lives of those in his community who are too destitute or stubborn to move.

**Appearance** An elderly ysoki in a threadbare flight suit covered in grease, Groovelock looks like he hasn't had a good night's sleep in his entire life.

**Personality** Crotchety, remorseful, skeptical

**Penalty** Offering to help or save Groovelock by helping him leave the settlement causes him to cut the meeting short, reducing it to 2 rounds instead of 3.

**Influence Skills** The skills the PCs can use to Influence the NPC are listed here with their DCs, in order from the lowest DC (the skill that works best] to the highest DC. if a skill isn't listed but a player gives a strong narrative explanation for using it, you can add it as an appropriate DC (usually the highest listed DC]. Diplomacy should usually be on this list, but should rarely be the best skill to Influence an NPC. in order to encourage and reward using Discover to learn and cater to an NPCs interests.

Influence Thresholds The number of Influence Points required to Influence the PC, and the benefits for meeting them. Some NPCs might have multiple influence thresholds, granting the PCs additional benefits or favors as they cross more thresholds. \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Resistances** Some NPCs are resistant to certain tactics, biased against certain types of people, or can get defensive when a certain topic comes up. Any of these makes it harder for a PC to convince them. For instance, an NPC might find flattery inane, dislike technomancers, or bristle at any mention of their ex-spouse. Typically, an NPC's resistance increases the DC of the associated check to Influence by 2 (or 5 for stronger resistances], but it could have farther-ranging consequences, such as losing Influence Points or angering the NPC enough that attempting to Influence them again is impossible.

Weaknesses Most NPCs have at least one weakness that clever and observant PCs can use to their advantage, whether it's a deep-seated insecurity, a desire for power, a favorite hobby, a bias toward a certain group, or a hidden secret the PCs could threaten to expose. When a PC incorporates an NPCs weakness, it typically decreases the associated Influence check's DC by 2 (or 5 for stronger weaknesses), but it could have farther-ranging effects, such as gaining automatic Influence Points or even automatically influencing the NPC regardless of how many Influence Points the PCs have achieved so far.

After the influence stat block, you might want to list important information to help you roleplay the NPC and incorporate the NPC into your influence encounter. You can list any of the following details that are relevant to your NPC: their background (a brief bio focusing on information relevant to the encounter], appearance, personality [this can just be a list of adjectives), affiliations, public goals, hidden agendas, or the penalty for antagonizing the NPC (or possibly for failing to Influence the NPC. depending on the way you structure the encounter).

For example, you might decide to create a hacker NPC who's hiding out in a nearby cybercafe while she toys with the party using fake infosphere accounts. Maybe you decide she's a member of Syzygy, a group that targets corporations and leaks important information to the public, who has been taking on odd jobs for a local crime boss.

The hacker was hired by an enemy to interfere with the party, but she might switch sides for an opportunity to get her hands on corporate secrets the PCs could help her access. You determine the NPCs goal is to hinder the PCs'

progress in their adventure, but her hidden agenda is taking down corporations.

If your encounter is occurring on Verces, you might decide the hacker NPC is verthani with cybernetic augmentations and that her personality is honest, sarcastic, and smug. Now that you know who she is, it's time to determine how the PCs might interact with this new NPC.

# SETTING DCS

When setting DCs, it's often good to start with a noncombat level or "social lever for the NPC and set their DCs accordingly. Use the DC adjustments from page 53 just like you normally would. A good starting place is setting the NPCs Will modifier, then taking that DC and adjusting it for skills that are more or less likely to work.

For instance, for a 3rd-level challenge, you might give an NPC a +12 Will modifier and use 22 as the base DC. You might say that's the DC for Diplomacy but then determine that the NPC is difficult to intimidate, and so you apply the hard DC adjustment to make the Intimidation DC 24.

Returning to the example above, maybe you also determine that the hacker NPC loves talking about vidgames, resulting in an incredibly easy DC adjustment to get DC 12 for Vidgame Lore. To represent her judgmental nature, you might decide the hacker isn't easily impressed by others' technical prowess and make the check to show off with Computers challenging, applying the hard DC adjustment to make the Computers DC 24.

### ■ **RUNNING AN INFLUENCE ENCOUNTER**

When running an influence encounter, let the PCsbe creative and use a diverse set of skills whenever possible. Be open to improvisation, and change the structure of the encounter if something interesting presents itself. The PCs set the pace and choose with whom they interact. It's up to you to make sure every NPC is distinct, react to the PCs' interactions with the NPCs, and lend overall structure to the encounter by making sure it feels like a living, breathing event rather than just a series of skill checks.

Think about how the number of rounds of a social encounter relates to the overall event. For instance, if you have a four-course brunch that takes 6 rounds, you could have 1 round for introductions before the food arrives, 1 round for each of the courses, and 1 last round of conversations after the final course. NPCs might filter in and out or become unavailable for conversations as they are occupied by various tasks, or become particularly eager to engage a PC. That sort of change helps make the NPC feel a bit more real and helps break up any repetition in your encounter.

# **| EXPERIENCE POINTS**

An influence encounter is typically worth the same amount of XP asa moderate combat encounter of its level would be.

**BUILDING GAMES**

**VICTORY POINTS**

**ANACHRONISTIC ADVENTURES**

# *STARFINDER*

Information is a powerful tool and an invaluable reward. While firsthand experience and Recall Knowledge checks are useful for piecing together clues, the research subsystem provides a way for the PCs to discover important information while challenged by a time limit or other interesting twist

In the research subsystem, PCs accumulate Research Points and learn new information or gain other benefits upon reaching specific thresholds. This subsystem is great for granting PCs more in-depth pieces of information as they continue to explore an area at large. Here, time passes in rounds spanning anywhere from 10 or so minutes to a full day. Each round, the characters use the Research exploration activity to gain Research Points (RP). As time passes and the party earns more RP, they gain knowledge and rewards, but also might face consequences or events. Some of these events might interrupt the round with a different kind of encounter [disrupting the Research activity], such as a social encounter with a lonely artificial intelligence or a combat encounter against a malfunctioning security robot.

Research challenges work best when the PCs face a time constraint, rival research group, or other form of external condition that presents additional pressures—if the PCshave all the time in the world to safely investigate a database or ruin, you can usually simplify things to a simple skill check since the PCs are free to keep rolling until they uncover everything there is to find.

# BUILDING A RESEARCH CHALLENGE

A research challenge has two components: the library, which is an area containing the various research checks PCs need to attempt to learn about the topic [as well as obstacles that the PCs face while doing so); and a research stat block, which details the topic being researched and contains the information, rewards, and additional complications that happen when the PCs reach certain thresholds of RP.

# DESIGNING THE LIBRARY

"Library'<sup>1</sup> is the general term to designate the setting of the PCs' research. Despite the name, it doesn't necessarily consist of a collection of books. It could be a computer database, astral memory palace, a club where the party questions the other guests, or even an infosphere. In many ways, designing a library is similar to designing any other adventure locale, with various rooms or other areas, each with its own complications for the PCsto overcome. If your library has a tactical map, these are likely hazards or encounters with hostile creatures; if your library is cinematic, especially if it's a virtual space, these might instead be skill-based or social encounters with NPCs.The PCs might even need a skill check or the Hacking subsystem [page 222) to gain access to the library.

Throughout the library, you'll place research checks. These describe the task that the party is doing to Research-asking in a chat room, skimming books, chemically testing samples, or talking to a stubborn administrator-and a number of skills

and DCs the party can use with the Research activity, in order from the lowest DC [the skill that works best) to the highest DC.

#### **RESEARCH**

#### **CONCENTRATE | EXPLORATIOH**

You comb through information to learn more about the topic at hand. Choose your research topic, section of the library, or other division depending on the form of research, and attempt a skill check. The skills to use and the DC for the check depend on the specific research task, and the Research activity gains any traits appropriate to the type of research [such as linguistic when perusing books).

**Critical Success** You gain 2 RP.

**Success**You gain 1 RP.

**Critical Failure** You make a false discovery and lose 1 RP.

ft a skin isn't listed but a player provides a strong explanation for using it, you can add it at an appropriate DC (usually the highest listed DC). Computers and Infosphere Lore will often be listed in these entries when researching virtually, and Society is often used to navigate and decipher libraries of physical media, but it's good to know the party's skill repertoires in advance so every member has a chance to participate; perhaps the data is stored in an open-world survival vidgame, requiring a round of Survival or Vidgame Lore checks. A variety of skills and decisions, along with vivid descriptions, are key to an engaging and memorable research challenge, rather than just a string of die rolls by the technomancer or witchwarper.

Each research check also has a maximum RP value. After reaching this value, the PCs can't gain any more RP from that research check, so they'll need to find more locations for further research. You can use this to guide your PCs through your library by placing research checks where you want them to explore-an easy check near the entrance to introduce them to the topic is usually helpful! Be sure that your library provides enough RP for players to meet all the research thresholds in the stat block.

# DESIGNING THE STOT BLOCK

Once you've decided how your library and its research options are structured, it's time to build the stat block and set research thresholds for each topic. Thresholds are your opportunity to reward your PCs with intriguing new information (like the history of the planet they're on), tactical intel (such as a prototype robot's only weakness), uncommon or rare options (like item formulas of lost technology), or anything else. A mix of rewards is best! Backstory can be interesting but isn't much of a reward on its own, so it should appear only at the lowest thresholds.

Reaching thresholds can also change the state of the library, in the PCs' favor or otherwise. In a poorly managed database, the first threshold might simply be cable management to help subsequent checks, but in a haunted database, necrolinked undead might appear to check their feeds. Reaching a research threshold can do just about anything, but it should have impact.

You don't need to evenly space thresholds-you could require very few Research Points for crucial clues you want to ensure the PCs receive and a much larger number to reach the final threshold that grants a special reward. **m m**

Published adventures use stat blocks like the one below.

# **RESEARCH TOPIC'S NAME RESEARCH [LEVEL]**

**Research Checks** The checks PCs can attempt to conduct research listed alongside their locations, tagged with area codes or page references when appropriate. \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Research Thresholds Each threshold lists the number of RP required to reach it, followed by the effects for meeting that threshold. Thresholds are listed in order from first (requiring the fewest RP) to last (the highest threshold).

Beneath the stat block, you can list any events that occur based on timing rather than the PCs' RP total (such as a threatening message arriving on the third day), as well as any other important details.

## **SAMPLE RESEARCH CHECKS AND STAT BLOCK**

The PCs are sneaking around an Azlanti dreadnaught on a mission to steal the plans for a new weapon. Its a 7thlevel challenge.

**Aeon Locks:** Specially attuned aeon stones are required to access the secure data banks on the ship; **Maximum RP** 5; **Research Checks** DC 23 Arcana or Occultism to reattune the lock, DC 28 Thievery to steal the commander's aeon stone.

**Sabotaging Virus:** A worm hidden in the ship's data banks could provide information on the worm's creator or provide a backdoor into the system: **Maximum RP** 10; **Research Checks** DC 18 Technology Lore or Underworld Lore to discover who coded the virus, DC 23 Computers to decode the virus completely.

**Captive Scientist:** The engineer in charge of the weapon project is an unwilling captive who might give away useful information; **Maximum RP** 15: **Research Checks** DC 21 Deception or Intimidation to force the researcher to comply, DC 23 Crafting to get the researcher to excitedly explain his work.

# **THE SIHEDRON CANNON \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_RESEARCH?**

**| UNIQUE | MAGICAL | TECH |**

**Research Checks** aeon locks [hallways), sabotaging virus (data center), captive scientist (cabins) \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

- **5 Research Points** The PCs gain access to the database and learn that the weapon is controlled by aeon stones. They learn basic details about Azlanti magitech.
- **10 Research Points** The PCs find a virus in the servers that references the project, but accessing the servers without proper clearance transforms the attuned oeon stones into green sapient orbs that attack.
- **15 Research Points** The captive scientist reveals the original goal of his research was to create a portable generator.He refuses to explain how it was weaponized.
- **20 Research Points** The captive scientist explains how his helpful device was turned into the Sihedron Cannon. Replace the captive scientist's Crafting Research check with a DC 28 Diplomacy check to convince him that the PCs plan on stopping the weapon from being used.
- **30 Research Points** The captive scientist gives the PCs a unique armor upgrade to stop his own creation. In addition to functioning like an advanced force field, it deflects the charged particles of the Sihedron Cannon back into the gun, destroying it and dealing its weapon damage to anyone within 120 feet of the wielder. He also tells the PCs that the admiral of the fleet keeps the cannon aboard the aeon fortress that travels with the fleet. Unfortunately, the guards protecting the captive scientist are making their rounds and attack the PCs. The PCs must defeat the aeon guard commander and evacuate the ship, then destroy or steal the weapon to prevent the Azlanti Star Empire from ever activating the Sihedron Connon.
